title: REVISED Community Life, Infrastructure and Public Property (CLIPP) Committee Meeting
overview: The Village of Kronenwetter's CLIPP Committee met on July 6, 2026, conducting a public hearing on revisions to Chapter 382 (Nuisances) of the village code and unanimously voting to forward the updated ordinance language to the Village Board for approval. The committee also discussed a traffic study recommendation for the County Road XX and Terabonding Drive intersection and heard a public comment requesting a bike and pedestrian path along Highway X.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Roll call
Public Hearing - Chapter 382 Nuisances ordinance revision
Public Comment - Kim Mayer on Highway X bike/pedestrian path
Approval of June 1 CLIPP meeting minutes
Police Chief report
Fire Chief report (absent)
Public Works Director report
Old Business - Chapter 382 Nuisances revision vote
New Business - Traffic control at County Road XX and Terabonding Drive
Next meeting date, future agenda items, and adjournment

discussions.body: Village President Dan Joling provided an overview of the proposed changes, explaining the ordinance removes the word 'public' from 'public nuisance' to make it more legally enforceable, requires complaints to be verified by law enforcement or supported by quantifiable evidence to prevent neighbor disputes, and extends the abatement period from 24 to 48 hours. Police Chief Terry McHugh confirmed the ordinance is largely similar to existing language with wordsmithing changes. No members of the public offered comment during the hearing.

discussions.item: Public Comment - Highway X Bike and Pedestrian Path
discussions.body: Kim Mayer, a resident near the intersection of Highway X and Pleasant Drive, presented a seven-page petition with signatures collected door-to-door and at the YMCA, advocating for a dedicated bike and pedestrian path along Highway X from Holland Avenue at the YMCA south to the intersection of Highway X and Highway XX. She cited safety concerns for children on scooters, cyclists, and pedestrians sharing the road with fast-moving traffic, and noted that both Police Chief McHugh and County Highway Commissioner Kevin Lane expressed support, with Lane indicating grant money is available. Committee members asked clarifying questions about the route and indicated staff would research costs and grant options for a future agenda.

discussions.item: Approval of June 1 CLIPP Meeting Minutes
discussions.body: A motion was made by Patty [Tikowski] to accept the June 1 CLIPP meeting minutes, seconded by Paul [Mayjol]. The committee approved the minutes by voice vote with no opposition.

Chief McHugh reported that calls for service are on pace to reach 8,000 in 2026, up from 7,100 the prior year, with an uptick in disturbances, fights, and drug-related incidents. He noted that following Officer Don's resignation, the department accelerated hiring: a new officer with prior law enforcement experience completed field training early and will soon begin solo patrol, a second hire's background process has been expedited with a target start of next month, and a third position has been posted on Wilinget with interviews planned for August and a fall hire anticipated.

Public Works Director Greg reported that the new maintenance shed is expected to have concrete poured in August with completion by January. He noted that state legislation raised the public bidding threshold, which is beneficial given rising costs. Sanitary sewer flow remains elevated at approximately 400,000 gallons per day due to a wet spring and early summer, down from higher levels but still above the target of 300,000. He also noted road salt prices increased about $17 per ton from the state contract.

discussions.item: Chapter 382 Nuisances - Vote to Forward to Village Board
discussions.body: Following the public hearing with no public opposition, Garrett Lizney moved to pass the revised nuisance ordinance language to the Village Board for approval. The motion was seconded and passed unanimously on a 5-0 roll call vote with all members voting yes.

discussions.item: Traffic Control - County Road XX and Terabonding Drive
discussions.body: Public Works Director Greg explained that County Highway Commissioner Kevin Lane requested elected officials' input on whether to make the current temporary four-way stop at County Road XX and Terabonding Drive permanent. Staff recommended commissioning a traffic study before making a decision rather than either immediately supporting or opposing a permanent four-way stop. The committee reached consensus to recommend the county conduct a traffic study while keeping the four-way stop in place during the study period, and that recommendation will be forwarded to the county.

publicComment: One speaker offered public comment. Kim Mayer, a resident near Highway X and Pleasant Drive, advocated for construction of a dedicated bike and pedestrian path along Highway X from Holland Avenue at the YMCA south to the intersection of Highway X and Highway XX. She presented a seven-page petition with signatures gathered door-to-door and at the YMCA, cited safety concerns she personally witnessed involving children, cyclists, and pedestrians, and reported that both Police Chief McHugh and County Highway Commissioner Kevin Lane expressed support for the idea, with Lane noting grant funding is available. No public comment was offered at the nuisance ordinance public hearing.

title: Parks Committee
overview: The Village of Kronenwetter held its inaugural Parks Committee meeting on June 29, 2026, electing officers, reviewing committee duties, and launching into substantive planning for Bushka Park improvements and fundraising. The newly formed seven-member committee — two trustees and five citizen members — focused heavily on a roughly $150,000-$200,000 playground replacement project at Bushka Park, with a public fundraiser planned for a July 17 movie-under-the-stars event. The committee also scheduled a parks tour for Wednesday, July 8 at 4 p.m. and set a meeting cadence of the first and third Mondays of July and August at 4 p.m.

agenda.item: Call to Order and Pledge of Allegiance
Roll Call
Selection of Chairperson
Selection of Vice Chairperson
Chairperson's Comments
Committee Duties and Responsibilities Review
Kronenwetter 2026-2030 Outdoor Recreation Plan Review
Bushka Park Fundraiser (Movie Under the Stars / July 17 Event)
General Fundraising Ideas
Future Parks Tour Planning
Setting Future Meeting Dates and Times
Adjournment

discussions.body: Patty DeCaliskey nominated Trustee Erin Mishka for chairperson. No other nominations were offered. A voice vote was taken; the ayes carried with one abstention noted. Erin Mishka was elected chairperson and assumed the chair for the remainder of the meeting.

Patty DeCaliskey nominated Dan Lesniac for vice chairperson. A roll call vote was taken; Theresa Holowinsky, Dan Lesniac, Tim Stracota, Patty DeCaliskey, Elizabeth Vedvik, and Trustee Mishka all voted yes. The motion carried unanimously.

Jennifer Poirier reviewed the committee's governing ordinances, noting the seven-member composition, four-member quorum requirement, advisory-only role to the Village Board, and the committee's duration of up to one year expiring at end of April. Trustee Mishka was credited with developing the duties document. Mishka indicated she would forgo her committee pay so the budget could support a potential sixth meeting.

The committee discussed the recently completed outdoor recreation plan, with Chairperson Mishka highlighting that the plan's core recommendations begin on page 36 and cover all village parks. Members noted a survey of only 156 respondents out of 8,623 residents felt too narrow in scope. Dan Lesniac shared anecdotal evidence of Bushka Park's baseball field being used daily. The committee agreed the plan should serve as a guiding document while also gathering additional community input, and that Bushka Park playground replacement — already identified as a top priority — aligns with survey findings that updated equipment was residents' top concern.

discussions.item: Bushka Park Fundraiser (Movie Under the Stars / July 17 Event)
discussions.body: The committee discussed leveraging the July 17 movie-under-the-stars event, hosted annually by On Cue Solutions (Ronda), as a fundraising kickoff for the Bushka Park playground project. The current fundraising balance is approximately $5,000 toward a goal of $200,000, with the base playground quote from Lee's Recreation at approximately $150,000, and the higher goal intended to allow for a rubberized surface instead of ADA wood chips. Ideas discussed included a wishing-well style transparent pool for coin/cash donations with QR code cards, Patty DeCaliskey selling jewelry with a portion going to the park, Elizabeth Vedvik doing home-bakery sales, a potential 50/50 raffle through On Cue Solutions (who holds a raffle license), a large presentation on the movie screen showing equipment breakdowns and sponsorship levels, and potential naming rights for playground elements such as swings or slides. The committee discussed the need to develop formal donation tiers with recognition (e.g., plaques). Chairperson Mishka and Vice Chair Lesniac noted they would both be absent July 17; Patty DeCaliskey and others agreed to present the fundraising pitch.

The committee brainstormed a wide range of future fundraising concepts including a 5K run/walk or poker walk connecting village parks, a summer concert event at Towering Pines Park with food trucks and a beer tent, a fall block party potentially in partnership with Relocation bar (which holds a liquor license), a carnival-style event with kids' games, and partnering with the Lion's Club on a fall fest. Members also discussed adding a park fundraising component to National Night Out (August 4) including a tug-of-war between police and fire departments. Jennifer Poirier noted the village cannot independently hold a raffle without partnering with a licensed organization. The committee agreed to focus near-term energy on the July 17 event and an August concert event, tentatively discussed for around August 23.

The committee agreed to conduct a parks tour led by Village Administrator Greg, planned for Wednesday, July 8 at 4 p.m. meeting at the municipal center, with Thursday, July 9 at 4 p.m. as a rain date. The tour will cover approximately five parks, with the committee debating whether to visit all parks; leadership recommended focusing on the busiest five parcels first to avoid exhaustion, with additional park visits possible at future meetings. The tour will be open to community members.

discussions.item: Setting Future Meeting Dates and Times
discussions.body: The committee agreed to meet on the first and third Mondays of July and August at 4 p.m., mirroring the schedule of the clip committee and plan commission. Specific dates discussed were July 20, August 3, and August 17. Members noted that once the school year begins, Monday afternoon meetings would become difficult; the committee aimed to complete most of its work by end of August. Additional meetings could be added as needed on alternate days such as Thursdays.
Dan Lesniac moved to adjourn; Tim Stracota seconded. The motion carried on a voice vote with all ayes and no opposition.

title: Room Tax Commission Meeting
overview: The Wausau Room Tax Commission held a brief meeting on June 23, 2026, with a single action item: a $25,000 room tax funding request for the Domtar Dam modernization project. The commission approved the funding unanimously after a short discussion confirming the expenditure falls within the room tax charter as infrastructure reasonably likely to generate overnight stays.

agenda.item: Call to order and quorum established
Discussion and possible action: Room Tax funding request for Domtar Dam modernization - $25,000
Adjournment

discussions.item: Room Tax Funding Request for Domtar Dam Modernization - $25,000
discussions.body: The chair introduced the item, noting the Domtar Dam modernization is a large, expensive project and that neighboring communities with a vested interest in Lake Wausau are being asked to contribute as a match to a grant from the John Alexander Foundation. Alderman Neil expressed support in principle but raised a concern about whether infrastructure spending falls within the room tax commission's charter. The chair and a participant identified as Mary Ann Lewitsky addressed the concern, citing the Peckham Way Trail as a recent precedent and referencing item number four of the room tax statute, which allows funding for 'tangible municipal development reasonably likely to generate overnight stays.' The motion was made by Neil and seconded by Lewitsky, and the item was approved unanimously.

publicComment: No public comment was offered. Mary Ann Lewitsky participated remotely and provided informational comments in support of the funding request, and the mayor was noted as present in the room.
actionItems: Approve $25,000 room tax funding contribution toward the Domtar Dam modernization project as a match to the John Alexander Foundation grant

title: City Council Retreat Meeting Pt.2
overview: The Wausau City Council held a strategic planning retreat where council members shared their top priorities for the 2026-2028 term, focusing heavily on economic development, housing affordability, fleet facility construction, transit expansion, and youth retention. The meeting concluded with a motion, passed unanimously, directing staff coordinator Michelle to compile all priority input from sticky notes and dot-voting exercises into a digestible format for distribution by end of week, after which the council will formally act on identifying the top three priorities.

agenda.item: Opening remarks and explanation of priority-setting exercise with sticky notes and dot voting
5A - Council member input on five-year strategic plan priorities
Alderman Myers presents three priorities: fleet facility, stop buying unnecessary properties, economic development without taxpayer cost
Alder Neal (Lou) discusses strategic plan critique, TID/TIF education, branding, and tactical planning
Alder Kilian speaks on critical needs, housing affordability, and living wage jobs
Alder Tierney discusses revenue diversification, education on TIF, housing, transit partnerships
Multiple alders discuss civic engagement, compassion, resident communication, and neighborhood groups
Alder Trueblood and others discuss fleet facility, housing stock, transit expansion, and e-motos
Mayor Diny speaks on Wausau's rankings, transit director's new approach, school enrollment trends, and historical strategic plans
Alder Watson's emailed priorities read aloud: infrastructure asset management, regional collaboration, long-term capital planning
5B - Additional priorities absent from strategic plan, including tax bill transparency, PFAS litigation updates, greening initiatives
Discussion and possible action: identification of top three priorities via dot voting
Motion to have Michelle compile priority information and distribute by end of week; vote and adjournment

discussions.body: Alderman Myers and Alder Trueblood both strongly advocated for action on the fleet facility, with Trueblood noting he has been on council eight years and the issue has been discussed for six of them with no resolution. A former infrastructure committee member noted the city has already spent $100,000-$200,000 on building plans and that much legwork is done, offering to attend future meetings to provide history on the project. The consensus among speakers was that the fleet facility must be completed this term rather than waiting for a potential county site to open up.

Alder Neal delivered an extended argument that council members and the public fundamentally misunderstand TIF and TID tools, warning that characterizing developer incentives as 'taxpayer money going to rich people' is inaccurate and puts Wausau at a competitive disadvantage. Alder Kilian countered that high market-rate rents in new apartment buildings are unaffordable for workers earning $20-$22 per hour, and called for more education so residents understand how these projects are financed. Alder Trueblood advocated for housing at all levels including market rate, affordable, single-family, duplexes, and accessory dwelling units, noting Wausau's housing stock is old and land for expansion is limited. Mayor Diny noted that developers build what generates ROI and the city's role is to work with developers, not act as one.

Alder Neal argued forcefully that attracting and retaining youth should be a top-line strategic priority, stating 'if we don't have a plan and if we aren't actively doing everything we can to attract and retain our youth here, our schools are emptying out.' He noted Wausau school enrollment has declined from roughly 8,400 to 7,400 students since 2000. Alder Tierney raised the idea of things for teens to do that don't cost money and mentioned reviving a mayor's youth council. Mayor Diny referenced Bentonville, Arkansas as a model community that transformed itself into a destination through trail systems and amenities, reversing talent drain.

One alder raised the issue of profound civic disengagement, noting that council and committee meetings routinely have empty houses, YouTube analytics on city meetings are very low, and the city lacks a centralized, cohesive communication strategy across departments. Alder Kilian described success with a neighborhood group in her district that now draws a packed room monthly. Multiple council members discussed the need for plain-language videos explaining tax bills and budget, with one alder describing spending hours with city staff to understand the revaluation before being able to explain it to constituents.

Alder Trueblood called Metro Ride the one thing Wausau has that no other community has and said it must be leveraged. Mayor Diny noted new transit director Ron Schunky is taking a new approach to regional expansion—selling transit to neighboring communities based on benefit to them rather than cost—and cited a new Northern Valley route serving 40-plus riders per day as early progress. Alder Luken advocated for expanding public transportation routes and times to serve residents who cannot afford personal vehicles, particularly to connect workers to local and regional employers.

Alder Tierney raised the issue of Wausau taxpayers funding police and ambulance overtime for county-wide events such as the fair, Art in the Park, and the upcoming air show, and suggested Marathon County share those costs. Another alder responded that the overtime issue is more nuanced than it appears, citing meetings held with the mayor, police chief, fire chief, and DPW, and announced an HR committee meeting on July 13th where all department heads will be invited to address overtime on the record. Council members were invited to submit questions in advance.

Alder Tierney raised the lack of public follow-up on the city's PFAS lawsuits, noting residents don't know what settlement money has been received or how it's being used. The city attorney confirmed payments have been received from 3M, Tyco, BASF, and DuPont over a settlement structure that will continue for nine years, and that a special needs claim is still pending for this summer. The attorney stated no settlement money has been allocated to anything yet and committed to preparing a confidential litigation summary update. Council members requested a finance department accounting of how funds are tracked.

discussions.item: Priority Dot Voting and Motion to Compile Results
discussions.body: Council members placed dot stickers on posted priority items organized under strategic plan headers. Because the exercise produced a complex visual result rather than a clear top three, a motion was made that staff coordinator Michelle compile all the information and send it to the council president by end of week, at which point the council president would determine how to distribute it for formal action. The motion was seconded and passed unanimously with no opposition.

publicComment: One member of the public was present but did not offer formal comment. Alder Neal acknowledged the visitor and invited him to contact council members individually. The visitor briefly mentioned interest in greening the city and innovative environmental remediation approaches when acknowledged by council.

title: Committee of the Whole Meeting
overview: The Wausau School Board Committee of the Whole met on June 22, 2026, approving several action items including Wisconsin Academic Standards for the 2026-27 school year, WIAA membership renewals, a $420,000 EFIS wall replacement contract at South Mountain Elementary, and authorization to use equipment auction proceeds for buildings and grounds equipment purchases. The board also received an informational AGR schools data presentation showing generally positive trends in math and literacy outcomes, and recognized the retirement of a 32-year district employee.

agenda.item: Approval of audit of bills
Recognition of Nol Torson retirement (32 years) and announcement of Jen Bonnke as new supervisor of financial services
Audit of bills approval
Annual notice of adoption of Wisconsin Academic Standards for 2026-27 school year
Annual renewal of WIAA memberships for Wausau East, Wausau West, and WAVE
AGR (Achievement Gap Reduction) schools semi-annual data review - second semester outcomes and continuous improvement plans
South Mountain Elementary EFIS wall system replacement bid approval

Special meeting: consent agenda approval (appointments, staff changes, retirements, leaves of absence)

discussions.body: Miss Channel announced the retirement of Nol Torson after 32 years with the Wausau School District, expressing pride in his service. She also announced that Jen Bonnke would be taking his position as supervisor of financial services. The board chair noted Torson is a quiet individual and did not put him on the spot to speak.
The board was asked to approve the audit of bills as presented. A motion was made, seconded, and passed unanimously with no discussion or public comment.

discussions.item: Annual Adoption of Wisconsin Academic Standards for 2026-27
discussions.body: Dr. Katie Cowwell and Miss Shell presented the annual notice, explaining the standards were created in 1988 and that Wisconsin Act 55 (2015) requires annual board approval. They noted DPI plans to review standards for dance, art, world languages, and environmental literacy this fall, and that there are now 25 content standards including new additions for art and design and WIDA English language development. The motion to approve passed unanimously.
The board considered annual renewal of WIAA memberships for Wausau East, Wausau West, and WAVE. No questions were raised. The motion to recommend approval to the full board passed unanimously.

A large team including district administrators, literacy coordinator Sandy, and building principals Jen Davidson (JD Jones), Phil Beckham (JD Jones associate principal), and Matt Schilling (Thomas Jefferson associate principal) presented spring data for Franklin, JD Jones, and Thomas Jefferson elementary schools. Math scores showed a slow uphill trajectory since fall 2023-24 with AGR buildings at approximately 62-64% at benchmark; kindergarten and first grade showed strong gains using the new Forefront number sense screener in its first year of use. Literacy data showed improvement over the prior year though some grade levels showed slight declines. Board member discussion focused heavily on classroom management after a board member shared a story of a newer teacher who didn't know she was empowered to firmly address disruptive behavior; administrators confirmed professional development on classroom management is planned including on the first day back. Board members also questioned assessment overload and spring testing fatigue, with staff acknowledging assessment windows would be adjusted. This item was informational only with no action taken.

discussions.item: South Mountain Elementary EFIS Wall System Replacement
discussions.body: Buildings and Grounds Director Ryan Hermansky presented the need to replace the EFIS (stucco) wall system at South Mountain Elementary, citing design defects identified through engineering assessment. He recommended replacing the system with 20-gauge steel panels, the same approach successfully used at Horace Mann last fall. Four bids were received and Jackson Associates, who also completed the Horace Mann project, was the low bidder at $420,000 with work planned for November 2026. A board member asked whether Jackson was responsible for the original faulty installation; Hermansky clarified Jackson did the repair work at Horace Mann, not the original installation. The motion to recommend full board approval of the bid and contract passed unanimously.

discussions.item: District Equipment Auction and Proceeds Designation
discussions.body: Hermansky requested board approval to hold an online auction through Wisconsin Surplus of obsolete district equipment including old lawnmowers, an automated mower blade sharpener, and a log splitter, and to direct the proceeds back to buildings and grounds for equipment purchases rather than the general fund. He estimated proceeds of $30,000-$60,000. A board member asked about consolidated school furniture; Hermansky clarified most was redistributed to schools or disposed of. The motion to recommend full board approval for auction proceeds to be designated to buildings and grounds for 2026-27 equipment needs passed unanimously.

A special meeting was called immediately following adjournment of the Committee of the Whole. The consent agenda covering appointments, additional staff, replacement staff, contract increases, separations, resignations, contract decreases, terminations, leaves of absence, and retirements was approved unanimously without discussion.

title: Village Board Meeting
overview: The Kronenwetter Village Board held its June 22, 2026 meeting, approving several key items including a $350,000 assessment services contract with Associated Appraisal, two budget amendments, and resolutions creating a special parks committee. The board also convened in closed session and voted to release an investigative report's executive summary in unredacted form while making the full report available in redacted form. Village Administrator was noted as being on vacation and not present.

discussions.body: Public Works Director Greg Allman reported that Lift Station 8 is progressing well and ditch regrading is underway at Kronenwetter Drive. He noted that a section near South Divide Junior Bridge does not meet depth and elevation standards and will be redone at the developer's expense, requiring Kronenwetter Drive to be reduced to one lane for approximately one to two weeks. The board indicated residents would be notified before the work begins.

The consent agenda included operator licenses for Territ Krasy Rushinsky and Christine Beck, minutes from the June 8 Village Board Meeting, June 9 Special Village Board Meeting, and the May 26 check register and credit card transactions. Trustee Mishka moved to approve and Trustee Coil seconded. Approved unanimously 7-0 on roll call.

Greg Allman explained this is an annual report required to be submitted to the DNR covering the sanitary system, maintenance programs, and financials. Trustee Mortensen moved to approve and Trustee Mishka seconded. Approved unanimously 7-0 on roll call.

discussions.item: Resolution 2026-009 - Creating a Special Parks Committee
discussions.body: Trustee Aaron Mishka presented the resolution, noting duties and responsibilities had been drafted. Trustee Lesniak moved to approve and Trustee Coil seconded. One trustee raised a question about whether the resolution included an end date; it was clarified by village ordinance that the committee automatically terminates after the next election in April 2027. The motion carried on a voice vote with no audible opposition.

discussions.item: Resolution 2026-010 - Appointing Citizens and Trustees to Commissions and Boards
discussions.body: President Joling noted the appointments were needed due to personnel movement and the death of a commission member. Trustee Mishka moved and Trustee Stowell seconded. A trustee thanked those stepping up to fill roles and noted one citizen member had to be persuaded but came to the meeting. Motion carried on voice vote.

discussions.item: Assessment Reevaluation Services Agreement - Associated Appraisal
discussions.body: Finance Director John presented three bids: Accurate Appraisal, Associated Appraisal of Appleton (current firm since 2017), and Municipal Group of Stevens Point. Staff and the APC recommended Associated Appraisal's Option 2 at $350,000 total ($180,000 for full revaluation plus $33,000-$37,000 annual maintenance over five years). John detailed why the village switched from Accurate Appraisal in 2017, citing over $1 million in assessment errors in 2015 requiring refunds to six property owners, as well as concerns about service response times and accuracy. Associated Appraisal's accuracy ratings posted to the Department of Revenue were also compared favorably. The board noted tax-exempt parcel review would be pursued. Trustee Lesniak moved to enter into a contract through end of 2031 for $355,000 and Trustee Mishka seconded. Approved unanimously 7-0 on roll call.

discussions.item: 2026 Budget Amendment No. 1 - DPW Truck Purchase
discussions.body: Finance Director John explained that the DPW's truck had a transmission failure that would have cost over $10,524 to repair, prompting an emergency vehicle purchase. The budget amendment transfers $35,000 from the Capital Projects Fund to the Capital Equipment Replacement Fund and appropriates $49,559 net of trade-in value for the 2015 Dodge truck replacement, purchased one year ahead of schedule. Trustee Coil moved and Trustee Solheim seconded. Approved unanimously 7-0 on roll call.

discussions.item: 2026 Budget Amendment No. 2 - Special Parks Committee Funding
discussions.body: Finance Director John presented a plan to fund the newly created Special Parks Committee by reducing APC meeting budget from 18 to 13 meetings (saving $6,046) and Zoning Board of Appeals from four to two meetings (saving $431), freeing up $1,077 to fund up to five parks committee meetings for the remainder of 2026. He noted there is no overall increase to the general fund budget. Trustee Lesniak moved and Trustee Coil seconded. Approved unanimously 7-0 on roll call.

discussions.item: Solisax Quartet Contract - National Night Out
discussions.body: The board considered approving a contract for the Solisax Quartet to perform at the National Night Out event on August 4, 2026. Jennifer noted the cost is covered by TDS Corporation as part of the village's 250th celebration Fourth of July programming, with no additional cost to the village. Trustee Stowell moved and Trustee Coil seconded. Approved unanimously 7-0 on roll call.

discussions.item: Closed Session and Action on Investigative Report
discussions.body: The board voted 7-0 to enter closed session under Wisconsin Stats. 19.85(1)(b) and (f) regarding personnel matters and an investigative report. Upon returning to open session, the board voted 7-0 to release the executive summary of the investigative report in unredacted form, and to make the full investigative report available in redacted form with personal identifiable information of witnesses protected.

title: Transit Commission Meeting
overview: The Wausau Transit Commission met on June 18, 2026, and approved two key items: sending a WisGo electronic fare collection contract to the Finance Committee for review, and allowing transit buses to serve as crowd barriers at the Wings Over Wausau airshow. The meeting also featured public comment from a former commissioner, Kathy Flurry, who disputed her removal from the commission by Mayor Doug Diny, and heard a director's update on ongoing technology upgrades including a new CAD/AVL system and the hire of a new deputy director.

agenda.item: Call to order and quorum confirmation

discussions.item: Public Comment - Kathy Flurry appointment dispute
discussions.body: Kathy Flurry, 915 North 2nd Avenue, addressed the commission disputing her removal from the Transit Commission by Mayor Doug Diny. She stated she signed an oath of office on October 22, 2025 with a term running through April 30, 2028, and was not informed of her removal until June 4, 2026, after a new community member (Christopher Weems) had already been appointed on June 2, 2026. She requested a written explanation and a copy of term limit policies, and asked for information on how to appeal the mayor's decision. A second speaker, Deborah Ryan of 702 Elm Street, corroborated parts of Flurry's account and alleged the city clerk had overstepped his boundaries in the appointment process. The chair thanked Flurry for her years of service but did not indicate any commission action would be taken.
A motion to accept the minutes of the preceding meetings was made by Weems and seconded by Watson. The motion passed unanimously with no discussion.

Transit Director Aaron (last name not stated) presented the WisGo electronic fare collection system, operated through Milwaukee Transit and used by multiple Wisconsin transit agencies, as a replacement for the current cash-only system. He outlined a one-time startup cost of $133,900 (to be covered by existing grant funds) and an annual fee of $24,260, and noted that fare increases would likely accompany the transition — adult fares from $1.75 to $2.00, senior fares from $0.85 to $1.00, adult monthly passes from $42 to $60, and senior monthly passes from $21 to $40. Commission members Alder Lukens and Alder Watson asked questions about card reloading, app integration, senior fare caps, and school ticket transitions. The director noted a driver was previously hospitalized after touching a fentanyl-laced transfer slip, citing driver safety as an additional benefit of going paperless. Weems moved to send the contract to finance for review; Alder Lukens seconded. The motion passed unanimously.

The director requested commission approval to drive idle express route buses to the airport to serve as crowd barriers for the Wings Over Wausau airshow (June 26-27), noting there would be no cost to the transit system and that it would provide advertising exposure. Alder Lukens moved to approve; Weems seconded. The motion passed unanimously. Commission members briefly discussed the possibility of future shuttle services for community events and suggested creating promotional materials or signage to display alongside the buses.

The director reported that CAD/AVL system installation on current fleet buses will begin next month, but the full system will not go live until new buses arrive in October-December, at which point everything will launch simultaneously. Scheduling software is expected to go live sooner and will be used to redesign staffing. A feasibility study for a new transit facility is ongoing, with the current building facing stormwater challenges. The director also announced the hire of a new deputy director starting July 6. Community member Mr. Meany expressed enthusiasm about the changes, noting he is a long-time bus rider.

Commission members proposed several future agenda items including a paratransit service overview for new members (requested by Alder Lukens), a presentation on e-bike compatibility with buses (the director confirmed current bike racks cannot support e-bike weight and there are battery safety concerns), marketing and outreach ideas including a bus presence at the August police and fire cookout (suggested by Mr. Wayne/Weems), and a potential resident fare discount tied to the new card system. The chair noted a council member had expressed interest in building a 'real system' in Wausau.

publicComment: Two speakers addressed the commission. Kathy Flurry (915 North 2nd Avenue) spoke about her disputed removal from the Transit Commission by Mayor Doug Diny, stating she had signed an oath of office for a term through 2028 but was told she could no longer serve after a new member was appointed. She requested a written explanation and appeal process information. Deborah Ryan (702 Elm Street), a 15-year neighborhood advocate, corroborated Flurry's account and alleged the city clerk acted inappropriately and unprofessionally in the appointment process, though she said she did not blame Flurry.

overview: The Wausau Board of Public Works met on June 16, 2026, approving several contracts and bids including a $303,888 award for East Parking Ramp repairs, a $578,572.35 pay estimate for lead service line replacement, and a change order for the downtown utility project. The board moved through six action items before entering closed session to consider pre-qualification statements for the North Fourth Street Lighting Project.

The low bid of $303,888 from Masonry Restorations Inc. was presented, with the next lowest being Building Restoration Corporation at $337,186. A board member noted the project budget was $300,000, making the low bid $3,888 over budget but within acceptable range. The net increase to the contract is $17,433.12 ($50,791.48 added minus $33,358.36 reduced). A board member confirmed the water department requested the upsizing and that the project is TID funded. The board voted unanimously to approve the change order.

discussions.item: Amendment 1 - Business Campus Trail East-West Connector contract (City of Wausau, WisDOT, MSA Professional Services)
discussions.body: TJ explained that a property owner along the planned trail route made an unreasonable purchase request and the city lacked eminent domain authority, requiring a trail redesign. The amendment totals $15,795.25, of which the city is responsible for only 20% due to 80/20 federal TAP program funding, though the full amount must be paid out before reimbursement. title: Board of Trustees
overview: The Village of Weston Board of Trustees held its regular meeting covering a wide range of topics including farmers market relocation feedback, fire department staffing challenges following a failed April referendum, liquor license renewals with compliance conditions, and road speed limit clarifications. Key decisions included referring the farmers market survey to the Parks Committee with the survey extended through June 30, and conditionally approving two liquor license renewals contingent on payment of outstanding debts.

agenda.item: Call to order, Pledge of Allegiance, and roll call
Public comments
Approval of May 18, 2026 board meeting minutes
Acknowledge board minutes from boards, committees, and commissions
Committee chair updates
Department reports (administrator, clerk, finance, fire, parks, police, public works)

Resolutions 2026-016 through 2026-018 (sewer utility, Plato Avenue reconstruction, Alderson/Gellet intersection)

Discussion and action on SAFER following April referendum result
Discussion and action on potential community engagement efforts and events

discussions.body: Three residents spoke. Grace Middle Street raised concerns about SAFER's $1.2 million fund balance, questioning why those funds weren't being used for firefighter staffing instead of seeking grants or a new referendum. Pam Zimmerman of 2550 Newport raised safety and parking concerns near Kennedy Park related to the farmers market, citing utility line risks, blocked sightlines from her driveway, and increased traffic. Jim Pel criticized omission of public safety meeting minutes from the packet, cited survey data showing 39% rate the new farmers market location poor or very poor and 57% prefer the former location, and questioned why the board continues borrowing for non-essential projects like golf fields while the fire department remains understaffed.

Trustee Herm reported that Public Works identified 15 million gallons of clear water being discharged into the sewer system monthly at a cost of about $20,000, totaling roughly $240,000 annually, and suggested penalties should be enforced. Fire Chief's representative reported six part-time firefighter positions will be offered, hail storm damage to department vehicles will cost only $2,500 total deductible, and two firefighters were injured during a water rescue that saved two people from a sinking boat on Lake Wausau. The board recognized the fire chief for receiving the state fire chief association award of the year. Public Works Director Michael reported a speed study on West Nav Avenue returned an 85th percentile speed of 48.8 mph, confirming the road can remain posted at 45 mph from Von Kennel to County J, with signs expected to be corrected by mid-week following road repair completion.

Consent items 34 through 39 were approved unanimously, covering vouchers, cigarette and tobacco license renewals, lodging license renewals, liquor license renewals, mobile home park licenses, and weight measures licenses. Item 40, appointment of a new agent for Reliance Fuel LLC doing business as Fuel on 60, was taken separately due to a name typo in the RFC; the board corrected it and approved the appointment of Sarah Gravine as agent, passed unanimously.

Resolution 2026-016 approving the 2025 CMAR for the Western Sewer Utility passed unanimously. Resolution 2026-017 authorizing special assessments for the Plato Avenue reconstruction from Pine Park Street to Alderson Street passed unanimously. Resolution 2026-018 for the Alderson Street and Gellet Avenue intersection project prompted questions about why the assessment rate was significantly higher ($8.61 per square foot) compared to Plato Avenue ($5.40 per square foot); Public Works explained the difference was due to economies of scale, with only two properties assessed on the Alderson project versus 40-plus on Plato. The resolution passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Farmers Market Feedback Survey and Parking Concerns
discussions.body: The board discussed survey results showing 39% of 322 respondents rate the Kennedy Park location poor or very poor, and 57% prefer the former location, with 75% citing parking as a problem. Trustees and staff debated parking management options including rope barriers, additional no-parking signage on East Newport, Joseph, and east side of Alderson Street, and extra police patrols on Saturday mornings. Parks staff noted chaotic parking during ball games is a separate and ongoing safety concern. The board agreed the Village Hall site is not a viable alternative due to insufficient square footage for vendors and parking. After withdrawing an initial motion to discuss, the board voted unanimously to refer the farmers market issue to the Parks Committee and extend the survey through June 30, with a possible reopening of the survey in August or September.

discussions.item: Liquor License Renewal - Hutches Kelly Club LLC
discussions.body: The board moved to conditionally approve the renewal of Hutches Kelly Club LLC's Class B fermented malt beverage and intoxicating liquor license for 2026-2027, contingent on payment of $662.76 in past-due utility amounts. Clerk confirmed the amount had already been paid by the time of the meeting, making the condition effectively satisfied. Motion passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Liquor License Renewal - RJT and Sons LLC
discussions.body: The board considered the renewal of RJT and Sons LLC's Class B licenses, which was complicated by $660 in past-due rehabilitation proof, a supplier bill of $91,825 past due for over 30 days, a history of fire code violations requiring repeated follow-up, and concerns about the owner's background. Trustee Beck raised concerns about approving a license for a business unable to pay its bills or license fee. Clerk noted that denying a license outright requires a formal process. The board amended the motion to include payment of the supplier bill as a condition; the amendment passed with one nay vote. The amended motion then passed 5-1, with the license conditioned on payment of past-due amounts, supplier bill, and proof of rehabilitation, all due before July 1.

discussions.item: SAFER Discussion Following April Referendum Failure
discussions.body: Administrator Gabbert outlined that a new referendum resolution would need to be filed with the county clerk by August 25 to make the November ballot. Finance Director Jessica explained the SAFER fund balance of approximately $1.3 million cannot be used for operations and is earmarked for capital projects; the board had discussed reducing it incrementally to $500,000-$600,000. With the TIF district closing, an estimated additional $503,000 in levy capacity may become available, potentially funding three additional firefighters. Trustees expressed skepticism about a second referendum at $600,000 given the April failure, with some suggesting a smaller, more realistic dollar amount or funding three positions through TIF proceeds and pursuing six later. Chief's deputy noted Rib Mountain, which shares 32% of SAFER costs, appears less enthusiastic about hiring six after the referendum failure. The board had extensive discussion but took no formal action, with budget discussions expected to continue.

Trustee Beck proposed community engagement initiatives modeled on Rib Mountain's well-received public event, including an annual open house at Village Hall and early morning coffee hours to accommodate residents on non-traditional schedules. The board received the idea positively and discussion began before the transcript ended, with Maloney noting the village had done similar events about 10-12 years ago.

publicComment: Three residents offered public comment. Grace Middle Street raised questions about SAFER's $1.2 million fund balance and why those funds weren't being directed toward firefighter staffing before seeking new grants or a referendum. Pam Zimmerman of 2550 Newport raised safety and property damage concerns about parking on East Newport and Alderson Street related to the farmers market, citing blocked driveway sightlines, utility line risks, and frequent turnarounds in her driveway, and questioned why the village hall site was not chosen for the farmers market. Jim Pel criticized missing public safety meeting minutes from board packets, cited the farmers market survey results as validating his earlier opposition to the Kennedy Park location, challenged the board to develop a concrete plan to fund the fire department before approving non-essential expenditures like additional golf fields, and called for better fiscal transparency in RFCs.

title: Public Health & Safety Committee Meeting Pt.1
overview: The Wausau Public Health & Safety Committee held its June 15, 2026 meeting covering a range of topics including a presentation on Bridge Street Mission's homeless shelter operations (119 unique guests since April 20 opening), a homelessness response framework from new county public health coordinator Megan Barnett, a detailed presentation on the police department's Flock Safety automated license plate reader program, and a rent abatement hearing for a tenant experiencing chronic window leaks for three years. The committee approved several routine items unanimously and directed staff on future reporting formats for homeless services data.

agenda.item: Public Comment
Item 2A: Approval of regular Public Health & Safety Committee minutes
Item 2B: Approval of special Public Health & Safety Committee meeting minutes
Bridge Street Mission shelter operations report - Craig Vincent and Jim Torstensson
County homelessness response framework presentation - Megan Barnett, Marathon County Public Health
Item 3A: Approval of various licenses and applications
Item 3B: 90-day extension request for True North Energy LLC liquor license, 4611 Stewart Avenue
Item 3C/3D: Review of municipal code chapter 1040 (bicycles, e-bikes) - stricken as already passed by council
Item 3E: Approval of City of Wausau Emergency Operations Plan
Item 3F: Approval of Fox Valley Fire Department mobile food preparation vehicle inspection program MOU
Item 3G: Urban Search and Rescue Response Agreement renewal 2026-2028
Item 3H: Discussion - Wausau Police Department staffing for downtown area
Item 4A: Flock Safety ALPR program overview, updates, privacy and security considerations - Captain Ben Graham
Item 4B: Tavern activities report and community outreach specialist report, May 2025
Item 5: Rent abatement hearing - 1418 North First Street, Apt. 105, tenant Viviani de Gomez vs. Parkmark Real Estate Services

discussions.body: One speaker, Deborah Ryan of 702 Elm Street, identified as a former alder and Westies neighborhood lead, addressed the committee. She raised concerns about gaps in homeless services on Sundays, the pending expiration of the city's ARPA-funded homeless coordinator position at year-end, the need for community surveys on services, and a specific case of a disabled individual receiving only $130/month and no housing assistance since November 2021. She also advocated for propane-powered municipal vehicles over electric given the climate.

discussions.item: Bridge Street Mission Shelter Operations Report
discussions.body: Craig Vincent and Director of Homeless Services Jim Torstensson reported that since opening April 20, the shelter served 119 unique guests (32% women), providing over 3,000 nights of shelter, with only five guests indefinitely dismissed for threatening behaviors. Community navigator staff helped three guests move to permanent housing and nine into recovery programming. Alder True Blood asked about denied service; Vincent confirmed the men's side had reached capacity and some were turned away, with overflow plans for cold weather. The women's recovery program also opened the prior week.

discussions.item: County Homelessness Response Framework - Megan Barnett
discussions.body: Megan Barnett, newly hired Marathon County public health coordinator, presented a foundational framework covering data collection, roles and leadership, strategic planning, and community engagement through year-end, with a goal of presenting a five-year strategic plan at a joint county board and city council meeting in December. She noted no one could quantify how many people are unhoused in the community due to lack of data, which she identified as a key barrier. Alder Lukans welcomed her warmly, saying the committee had 'been hoping for, wanting to get you for a long, long time.' No vote was taken; this was an informational presentation.

Regular minutes were moved by Alder Lukans, seconded by Alder Watson, and approved unanimously. Special meeting minutes were moved by Alder True Blood, seconded by Alder Watson, and approved unanimously. No discussion or corrections were raised.
Alder Watson moved to approve all license applications as noted in the staff memo, with no denials reported. Seconded by Alder Solonsky. Approved unanimously.

discussions.item: Item 3B: True North Energy LLC 90-day liquor license extension
discussions.body: Representatives Michelle Knox and Josh Kasinski appeared on behalf of True North Energy LLC for the former Wagner Shell station at 4611 Stewart Avenue, requesting an extension while the property undergoes renovation. The chair confirmed the target reopening date of September 18. Alder Watson moved to grant the extension to September 18; seconded by Alder True Blood. Approved unanimously.

Alder True Blood noted the ordinance had already been passed by the full city council on May 26 as part of the consent agenda. The chair struck these items from the agenda. A second item was similarly identified as already passed by council following discussion with the city attorney.

discussions.item: Item 3E: City of Wausau Emergency Operations Plan
discussions.body: Emergency Management Director Philip Brent Master presented the revised plan and identified several grammatical corrections needed, plus a substantive change on page 19: succession of authority was updated so that if the mayor is unavailable, the council president (an elected official) would take charge, replacing the previous plan which designated the police chief. Alder Watson moved to approve with the grammatical and content updates included; seconded by Alder Lukans. Approved unanimously.

discussions.item: Item 3F: Fox Valley Fire Department mobile food preparation vehicle inspection MOU
discussions.body: Chief explained the program allows food trucks to be inspected once by any participating department, with a sticker valid for one year, using NFBA national standards. Alder True Blood raised concerns about trucks passing inspection in one jurisdiction and later failing in another, and requested the division chief of prevention provide failure-rate data. Alder Watson suggested food trucks may warrant more frequent inspections than once annually given their mobile nature; Chief noted national NFBA standards address this. Moved by Alder Solonsky, seconded by Alder True Blood. Approved unanimously.

discussions.item: Item 3G: Urban Search and Rescue Response Agreement 2026-2028
discussions.body: Chief described this as the third renewal of the state contract for Task Force One urban search and rescue, covering training reimbursement and deployment costs for two department members trained in confined space, rope rescue, swiftwater rescue, and structural collapse response. Alder Solonsky confirmed training applies to both urban and rural structural collapse. Moved by Alder Solonsky, seconded by Alder Watson. Approved unanimously.

Captain Syler reported that the two downtown officer positions have continued flexing citywide since the shelter moved across the river, with no scheduling changes required. He noted a dramatic decrease in calls for service at Bridge Street Mission from April/May to June, crediting relationship-building with clients and staff. Alder Watson asked about neighborhood reception on the west side; Syler said early concerns from neighbors were addressed through proactive communication and that downtown business owners have been telling officers this spring and summer has been 'a breath of fresh air.' No vote taken; informational discussion only.

discussions.item: Item 4A: Flock Safety ALPR Program - Captain Ben Graham
discussions.body: Captain Ben Graham delivered a detailed presentation on the department's 20-camera automated license plate reader network, costing $50,000/year under a contract running through 2028. He highlighted 35 documented case successes, described privacy safeguards including 30-day data purging, monthly audit reviews, multi-factor authentication, and confirmed the department has opted out of nationwide data sharing and filters out federal immigration, ICE, DHS, FBI, and reproductive care search requests. He acknowledged three Wisconsin officers at other agencies were criminally charged for misuse of similar systems. Alder Watson noted the policy's human-review requirement before any enforcement action. Alder Slonsky pressed on whether partner agencies could submit false reasons for shared hot-list queries; Graham acknowledged the theoretical possibility but noted audit trails provide accountability. Alder True Blood, referencing other Wisconsin municipalities cutting ties with Flock, expressed appreciation for the presentation and guardrails. The committee requested Captain Graham return annually in January with an audit summary report; Graham agreed. No vote taken.

discussions.item: Item 4B: Tavern activities report and community outreach specialist report - May 2025
discussions.body: Captain Pauls reported all tavern businesses passed checks in May, with a couple of calls initiated by bartenders as hoped. Alder Watson asked whether Bridge Street Mission data could be incorporated into future monthly reports; Community Outreach Specialist Tracy confirmed it could. The chair indicated next month's agenda would include a discussion on future reporting format, potentially alternating monthly reports between Tracy and Bridge Street Mission, and possibly moving to quarterly reporting as suggested by Alder True Blood. Both reports placed on file.

discussions.item: Item 5: Rent Abatement Hearing - 1418 North First Street Apt. 105
discussions.body: Tenant Viviani de Gomez testified under oath that persistent window leaks have plagued her three-bedroom apartment since move-in in July 2023, requiring her to move furniture before every rain event, with water intrusion reaching outlets and causing mold on the ceiling. She pays $945/month and is current on rent. Property manager Joe Seagull of Paramark Real Estate testified multiple contractors had been engaged (Rip Mountain Glass, Ace Handyman, Quality Roofing, AA Seamless, Prestige Roofing), claimed repairs were attempted, and said he offered the tenant a no-transfer-fee move to an identical unit twice, which the tenant declined. City Inspector Adam Molzan testified he first inspected July 24, 2025 at the tenant's request, issued a municipal citation on August 13 for failure to properly repair leaking windows, and confirmed during a visit on June 10, 2026 water was still actively dripping inside the apartment with apparent mold in two ceiling locations. Molzan noted he received no repair documentation from the landlord. The transcript ends before the committee voted; the chair indicated the committee would adjourn to closed session to deliberate.

publicComment: One speaker offered public comment: Deborah Ryan, 702 Elm Street, former alder and Westies neighborhood lead. She raised concerns about Sunday gaps in homeless shelter services (Bridge Street Mission is closed Sundays with no alternatives), the upcoming expiration of the ARPA-funded homeless coordinator position at year-end, the need for client surveys similar to those used at Catholic Charities, a specific case of a homeless man with disability income of $130/month receiving no housing assistance since November 2021, and advocated for propane-powered municipal fleet vehicles over electric given Wisconsin's climate.
actionItems: Regular and special Public Health & Safety Committee minutes approved unanimously
All May 2026 license applications approved unanimously with no denials
True North Energy LLC liquor license extended to September 18, 2026 approved unanimously
City of Wausau Emergency Operations Plan approved with grammatical corrections and succession-of-authority update (council president replaces police chief in mayor absence) unanimously
Fox Valley Fire Department mobile food preparation vehicle inspection MOU approved unanimously; Chief to obtain failure-rate data from division chief of prevention for future report
Urban Search and Rescue Response Agreement 2026-2028 renewal approved unanimously
Captain Ben Graham to return in January annually to provide Flock Safety ALPR audit summary report to committee
Department opted out of Flock Safety nationwide data sharing as of May 2026; ICE, DHS, FBI, immigration, and reproductive care search requests already filtered

Chair to place on next month's agenda a discussion on format for future homeless services reporting (frequency, whether Bridge Street Mission reports separately or through community outreach specialist)
Rent abatement hearing for 1418 North First Street Apt. 105 testimony completed; committee to deliberate in closed session (outcome not captured in transcript)

votes.item: Regular and special Public Health & Safety Committee minutes
All May 2026 license applications
True North Energy LLC liquor license extended to September 18, 2026
City of Wausau Emergency Operations Plan with grammatical corrections and succession-of-authority update

title: Administrative Policy Committee Meeting
overview: The Kronenwetter Village Administrative Policy Committee met on June 15, 2026, taking several key votes including recommending Associated Appraisal of Appleton for a $355,000 reassessment contract and approving budget amendments for a DPW truck purchase and a new special parks committee. The meeting also included a review of the village's 2024 audit results and a five-year financial comparison presented by Finance Director John, who highlighted improving general fund trends while noting ongoing concerns about the TID1 fund deficit and accounting software configuration issues.

agenda.item: Call to order, Pledge of Allegiance, and roll call
Item 5F (taken out of order) - Assessment and Reevaluation Services Proposals
Public comment
Approval of minutes - May 19, 2026 APC meeting
Item 4D - 2024 audit review
Item 4E - 2024 comparison stats for past five audits
Item 5G - Solis Sachs Cortette musician contract for National Night Out
Item 5H - Budget Amendment 1: DPW truck purchase financing
Budget Amendment 2: New special parks committee funding
Consideration of items for future agenda and adjournment

discussions.body: Finance Director John presented three proposals received for reassessment and assessment services for July 2026 through December 2031: Accurate Appraisal at $240,900, Associated Appraisal (current vendor since 2017) at $350,000 for Option 2, and Municipal Group at $372,000 for an exterior evaluation option. John recommended Associated Appraisal Option 2 at $355,000, citing their 67 years of experience, 240 municipalities served, and satisfactory performance at Board of Review, and noted that if the village does nothing the state would intervene at roughly double the cost. He indicated there were unstated reasons the village switched from Accurate Appraisal in 2017. President Jolyne motioned to accept Associated Appraisal Option 2 for $355,000, seconded by Trustee Solheim; approved 3-0 as a recommendation to the Village Board.

Finance Director John summarized the 2024 audit, noting the village received an unmodified (clean) opinion, the best possible result, though auditors identified material weaknesses and significant deficiencies. John expressed frustration that auditors presented only the negative findings without discussing the financial numbers, and that a predecessor had been carrying an unreconciled ~$80,000 monthly adjustment that was rolled forward repeatedly. He also described discovering that the Counsil accounting software had been configured on a cash basis rather than accrual, causing mismatches between posted payments and actual bank disbursements; he and staff member Kim identified this issue and are working to correct it. No vote was taken; this was a discussion and informational item.

discussions.item: 2024 Comparison Stats for Past Five Audits (Item 4E)
discussions.body: John presented a five-year financial snapshot to provide context the auditors omitted, showing the general fund balance at 16.44% for 2024 versus a 15% policy minimum, recovering from a low of roughly 3% in 2023. He highlighted $18 million in cash and investments (largely unspent borrowed funds), a declining debt service fund balance projected to reach ~$30,000 by end of 2026 due to prior under-levying, and a growing TID1 fund deficit advancing from the general fund now totaling ~$2.7 million, which John said would be resolved once the anticipated distribution center development generates property tax increment. No vote was taken; committee members expressed appreciation for the presentation.

discussions.item: Solis Sachs Cortette Musician Contract - National Night Out (Item 5G)
discussions.body: The committee considered a $750 contract with Solis Sachs Cortette for a patriotic musical performance at National Night Out on August 4, 2026, to celebrate the US 250th anniversary, an idea previously raised by Trustee Sarnson. Finance Director Jennifer was not present but relayed that the cost would be covered by sponsorships with no cost to the village. Trustee Solheim made the motion and President Jolyne seconded; approved 3-0 as a recommendation to the Village Board.

Village Administrator Jim explained that a DPW truck suffered a transmission failure and was already slated for replacement next year; replacing the transmission at over $10,000 was deemed impractical. The board had previously approved the purchase; this item addressed how to fund it. John proposed moving $35,000 from the capital projects fund to the capital equipment replacement fund, with the net purchase cost after trade-in being $49,559, offset partly by savings from police squad car purchases. President Jolyne motioned and Trustee Solheim seconded the recommendation to concur with the finance director's budget adjustments; approved 3-0 by roll call vote.

discussions.item: Budget Amendment 2 - Special Parks Committee Funding (Item 5H continued)
discussions.body: Following the board's June 8, 2026 creation of a new special parks committee with five citizen members paid per meeting, John proposed a net-neutral budget realignment: reducing budgeted APC meetings from 18 to 13 and zoning board of appeals meetings from 4 to 2, redirecting those funds to cover up to five meetings for the five citizen members for the remainder of 2026 at $10 per attendee per meeting. Trustee Solheim motioned and President Jolyne seconded the recommendation; approved 3-0 by roll call vote as a recommendation to the Village Board.

publicComment: No public comment was offered.
actionItems: Recommend to Village Board approval of Associated Appraisal (Appleton) Option 2 contract for reassessment and assessment services at $355,000 for July 2026 through December 2031 - to be presented at next Monday's board meeting
Recommend to Village Board approval of contract with Solis Sachs Cortette for musical performance at National Night Out on August 4, 2026 ($750, covered by sponsorships)

Recommend to Village Board budget adjustments to fund DPW truck purchase: transfer $35,000 from capital projects fund (Fund 400) to capital equipment replacement fund (Fund 750), net purchase cost $49,559 after trade-in
Recommend to Village Board 2026 budget amendments realigning meeting allocations to fund new special parks committee citizen member compensation, net-neutral to general fund

Finance Director John and staff member Samantha to work on correcting accounting software (Counsil) from cash-basis to accrual-basis configuration and reconciling monthly payment mismatches before 2025 audit
2025 audit target completion: mid-to-late September 2026
Next APC meeting: July 21, 2026

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Finance and Human Resources Committee held its June 15th meeting, approving monthly financial reports and receiving an educational presentation from the finance department on its operations. The most significant action was reversing two prior motions from March regarding employee uniform policy and approving a new hybrid approach: employees may either keep their Cintas uniform rental service or opt out and receive $500 annually to purchase their own clothing, with staff to explore a shorter-term Cintas contract.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Roll call
Public comment
Item 4: Approval of minutes from May 18th meeting
Items 5-9: May budget status report, TIF 1 and TIF 2 detail reports, legal details for May, Public Service Commission annual report, State Municipal Financial Report
Item 10: Educational presentation - Finance Department overview
Item 11: Old business - Discussion and action on amendments to clothing allowance policy
New business, staff remarks, committee member remarks, and adjournment

discussions.body: A motion to approve was made by Daniels and seconded by Mynull (phonetic). No discussion occurred and the motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Items 5-9: May budget status report, TIF reports, legal details, PSC annual report, State Municipal Financial Report
discussions.body: Finance staff Jessica presented all five items together. Discussion arose around item 7 (legal details) about whether the committee still needed to review it monthly, with committee members suggesting it be brought to the full board to decide. Jessica noted the legal detail report was originally requested by members who wanted to monitor legal spending without searching the full check register. For item 8, Jessica and utility staff explained the PSC annual report in detail, noting 1,029 fire hydrants, 6,091 customers, a 2% rate of return, and approximately 10 miles of asbestos cement water main remaining, being replaced at about one mile per year. The committee acknowledged all five items via a motion by Mynull, seconded by Daniels, which passed unanimously.

Finance Director Jessica and staff members Song and Renee gave a detailed presentation on the finance department's operations, covering payroll (ranging from 38 to 64 employees per pay period, averaging $111,410 bi-weekly), accounts payable ($50,266,621 in total disbursements in 2025, with 2,420 checks and 47 ACH payments), accounts receivable ($1.2 million billed in 2024), the multi-step invoice approval process, cash management, and required state reporting. Jessica emphasized the importance of asking questions about financial reports, illustrating with a cautionary example from a South Area Fair Emergency District that had hidden a $574,000 intergovernmental payable. Committee members asked about Excel-related human error risks, and Jessica clarified the department uses a software-integrated 'My Excel' tool that pulls directly from the Casselle financial system.

discussions.item: Item 11: Old business - Clothing allowance amendments
discussions.body: Public Works Director Michael presented a revised recommendation on the employee uniform policy, walking back the committee's March decision to cancel the Cintas contract and offer $400 plus purchase a washer and dryer. Michael said employee morale concerns and the complexity of a one-size-fits-all policy led him to recommend a hybrid approach: employees may keep Cintas service or opt out and receive $500 annually to purchase their own clothing, with the election made each January. The committee also discussed pursuing a shorter-term or month-to-month Cintas arrangement rather than a new three-year contract given past billing disputes. Committee member Barb moved to first rescind the two prior motions from March (canceling Cintas and approving $400 plus washer/dryer), which passed unanimously, then moved the new hybrid policy, which also passed unanimously.

publicComment: No public comment was offered. The chair confirmed no one was present in the audience or online at the start of the meeting.
actionItems: Rescinded prior March motions to cancel Cintas contract and to set clothing allowance at $400 with purchase of washer and dryer

Next Finance and Human Resources Committee meeting scheduled for Monday, July 20th at 4:30 p.m.

title: Special Village Board Meeting
overview: The Kronenwetter Village Board held a special meeting on June 9, 2026, primarily to interview a candidate for the Community Development Director position in closed session. Following the closed session, the board voted unanimously to authorize the village administrator to make a job offer to candidate Mason Sandwick within parameters set during the closed session. This decision is significant to residents as it fills a key community development leadership role.

The motion passed on a 4-0 roll call vote, with Trustees Coil, Lysney, Borgensen, and Mishka voting yes; Trustees Stoll and President Jolene were noted as present or absent during this vote.

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Wausau School Board held its regular June 2026 meeting, approving seven motions covering the preliminary 2026-27 budget, employee handbook updates, meal price changes, athletics/activities code revisions, policy updates, an HVAC/roofing contractor selection, and a debt defeasance strategy that will bring the district's total long-term interest savings to just over $29 million. The meeting also featured presentations on the art department's three-year goals and the district's summer learning programs.

agenda.item: Roll Call and Pledge of Allegiance
Art Department Presentation - Three-Year Goals and Curriculum Alignment
Public and Student Comment
Consent Agenda - Personnel, Minutes, Bills, and Donations
Motion: Preliminary 2026-27 Budget Approval
Motion: 2026-27 Employee Handbook Updates
Motion: Paid Meal Price Update for 2026-27
Motion: Athletics and Activities Code Revisions

Motion: Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code

Summer Learning Program Update

discussions.item: Art Department Presentation - Three-Year Goals and Curriculum Alignment
discussions.body: The district's art coordinator, in her second year, presented the art department's three-year goals focused on amplifying arts reach, creating shared K-12 skill experiences, and meaningful vertical alignment between grade levels. She described the creation of an art continuum task force involving elementary, middle, and high school educators who identified priority standards, and a department-wide book study on teaching meaning in art making. Board member Lance Trupop expressed support, noting the value of consistent experiences as students move into middle school. The coordinator also announced an upcoming partnership with the Leigh Yawkey Woodson Art Museum to give art educators opportunities to make art themselves.

discussions.item: Consent Agenda - Personnel, Minutes, Bills, and Donations
discussions.body: The consent agenda included personnel actions, minutes from the May 11 and May 18, 2026 sessions, payment of bills, budget status, investment report, and donations from multiple community organizations including CW Fly Bags, Hub Central Collision, King Buffet of Wausau, Lee Insurance, Peace United Church of Christ, Safe Kids of Marathon County Coalition, Wausau Breakfast Optimist Club, ENT and Allergy Associates, and Cloverbeltt Credit Union. A motion was made, seconded, and approved unanimously with no discussion.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, had previously presented the preliminary budget at the May committee meeting. The board was asked to approve the initial budget to allow 2026-27 expenditures to proceed before final budgets are set. The motion was made, seconded, and passed unanimously with no board discussion at this meeting.
Tabitha Gundrum had presented employee handbook updates at the May committee meeting. The board approved the recommended changes unanimously with no additional discussion at this meeting.
Karen Fox had previously presented proposed meal price changes at the May committee meeting. The board approved the proposed changes unanimously with no discussion.

Dr. John Tomsky and BJ Brandt had presented suggested changes to the athletic and activities code at the May committee meeting. The board approved the revised code unanimously with no additional discussion.

discussions.item: Policy 9270 - Home-Based Private or Tribal Schooling and Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code
discussions.body: Dr. John Tomsky had presented proposed changes to both policies at the May committee meeting. Both policies were moved, seconded, and approved unanimously in separate votes with no board discussion.

discussions.item: Rearview HVAC and Roofing Project - Myron Construction Corporation
discussions.body: Ryan Armansky, director of buildings and grounds, had presented this project at the May committee meeting. The board approved moving forward with Myron Construction Corporation as the general contractor unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: 2026-27 Debt Defeasance Strategy Presentation and Adoption
discussions.body: Eric Cass from PMA Financial presented the annual debt defeasance strategy, explaining that the district would place excess levy funds into an irrevocable escrow trust targeting the longest-maturity, highest-coupon debt from a 2022 issuance, eliminating nearly three years of debt service through 2039-2040 and generating approximately $9.6 million in savings. Combined with prior years' defeasances dating back to 2017-18, total long-term interest savings would reach just over $29 million. Board member Nick Casher asked whether repeatedly prepaying debt affects the district's standing with bond investors; Cass explained that the callable feature is disclosed upfront and investors price in the possibility of early redemption. Another board member noted the strategy also helps smooth mill rate fluctuations and generates additional state equalization aid, projected at $5.7 million, though Cass cautioned that figure carries volatility. The motion was approved unanimously.

Rob Phelps, principal at Horace Mann and summer learning coordinator, and Vicki Christensen, out-of-school learning coordinator, began presenting the district's summer learning programs. Phelps described the morning elementary program, structured as 'transition to' grade-level programs focusing on math, ELA, and encore rotations, and a middle school transition program combining school forest visits with academic intervention. The transcript ends mid-presentation before the full update was completed.

publicComment: No public comment was offered. The board noted that a written comment submission option exists on the district website but encouraged residents to email the board directly instead.
