title: Village Board Meeting
overview: The Village of Kronenwetter Board held its June 8, 2026 meeting, covering a 2024 audit presentation revealing six financial deficiencies including material weaknesses in bank reconciliations and segregation of duties, and taking several significant votes including appointing Mary Solheim to a vacant trustee seat, approving a $49,558.50 truck purchase for public works, awarding a $799,780 municipal garage contract, and voting to create a special parks committee. The audit findings - including delayed bank reconciliations not completed until February 2026 and numerous unreconciled accounts - drew sharp criticism from public commenters who noted the village has been going backward on financial controls.

agenda.item: Call to Order, Pledge of Allegiance, Roll Call
2024 Audit Presentation - Discussion and Possible Action (Item 5)
Public Comment
Staff Reports - Police Chief and Fire Chief
Administrator Report - Mr. Davell (storm damage, transfer switch repair)
Finance Director Report - Mr. Jacobs (five-year financial snapshot)
Consent Agenda Items H through M
Item 9 - Purchase of New 3/4-Ton Crew Cab Truck for Public Works
Item 10 - Appointment of Village Board Trustee
Municipal Garage Building Proposals
Kronenwetter Park Committee Formation

discussions.body: Auditors Amber and Yelsky and Subert Randall from the village's audit firm presented six findings: two material weaknesses (segregation of duties, failure to prepare the annual financial report internally) and four additional deficiencies including late bank reconciliations (the year-end 2024 reconciliation was not received until February 2026 and January 2025 not until May 2026), lack of journal entry documentation, unreconciled non-cash accounts, and audit adjustments required. The auditors noted the village received an unmodified (clean) opinion but only after extra procedures. They projected beginning the 2025 audit in mid-to-late September 2026 contingent on timely bank reconciliations. Board members thanked the auditors and acknowledged the need for a corrective action plan, with Administrator Davell and Finance Director Jacobs indicating they are working on improvements including potentially hiring an additional finance staff member.

discussions.item: Public Comment - Audit and Other Topics
discussions.body: Public commenter Bernie Kramer criticized the village's financial management, noting the number of audit deficiencies has grown from three to six and calling out repeated findings that have not been corrected, stating 'we've been going backwards.' He questioned how the village expects to gain citizen trust. Tim Stercora spoke in support of the already-approved Leah Road trails project, arguing it has been delayed by 'lame excuses for about four years' and that water utility safety concerns about proximity to walking paths are overblown. Patty Tulkowski spoke in support of Garrett Lezmi as a candidate for the vacant trustee position, citing his professional engineering background and service on the CLIP committee.

Finance Director John Jacobs presented a five-year financial snapshot highlighting key metrics relevant to upcoming borrowing. He reported the 2024 unassigned general fund balance was $931,000 (16% of budget, above the 15% policy threshold), cash and investments at $18 million (inflated by $7.8 million in borrowed capital project funds), and general obligation debt at $13.5 million or 18.1% of the debt limit. Jacobs noted TID1 carries a $2.6 million deficit advancing from the general fund but predicted it will reverse once the distribution center generates tax increment. He also flagged that the debt service fund had been incorrectly over-funded for years. Jacobs stated he is confident in achieving a September 2025 audit start date and confirmed the village is not facing bankruptcy despite public claims to the contrary.

discussions.item: Consent Agenda Items H through M
discussions.body: Trustee Coil moved and Trustee Mortensen seconded approval of consent agenda items H through M. There was brief discussion confirming a vendor had all necessary documentation included. The motion passed unanimously on a voice vote.

discussions.item: Purchase of New 3/4-Ton Crew Cab Truck for Public Works
discussions.body: Public Works Director Holman explained that a 2015 pickup truck's transmission failed, with repair estimates of $8,000-$10,000 on a truck worth only $12,000-$14,000 and already slated for replacement in 2027. Staff recommended purchasing a 2026 GMC Sierra 2500 for $49,558.50 over a competing Dodge bid. Finance Director Jacobs outlined a four-step funding plan using existing capital project and equipment replacement funds without new borrowing. Trustee Coil moved and Trustee Mishka seconded approval. The motion passed 6-0 on a roll call vote with all trustees and the president voting yes.

Five applicants addressed the board: Missy Swanson (social worker/therapist), Matthew Heldenbrand (former planning commission and county board member), Mary Solheim (human services director with large budget management experience), and Alex Veddick (former village trustee); Chris Johnson was absent. President Jolene nominated Mary Solheim, seconded by Trustee Stoel. Trustee Mishka nominated Alex Veddick, seconded by Trustee Mortensen. On the roll call vote for Solheim: Trustee Coil yes, Trustee Lesniane yes, Trustee Mortensen yes, Trustee Mishka no, Trustee Stoel yes, President Jolene yes. Solheim was appointed 5-1.

Public Works Director Holman presented six design-build RFP responses for a six-bay equipment garage to house police vehicles, parks equipment, a sewer truck, and a hoist truck. Both the CLIP committee and the Utility Committee recommended S.D. Allen Becker of Athens, Wisconsin at $799,780, noting they were the only firm that visited the site in person. Funding is one-third from sewer utility and two-thirds from capital project funds with no borrowing required. Trustee Mishka moved and Trustee Mortensen seconded approval of the S.D. Allen Becker bid at $799,780. The motion passed unanimously 6-0 on a roll call vote.

Trustee Mishka proposed creating a parks committee after three residents expressed interest at a May bike-and-walk health event survey. Discussion centered on whether to create a subcommittee of CLIP or a separate special committee; Administrator Davell noted the staffing burden of additional meeting minutes. After clarification that village ordinances use the term 'special committee' rather than 'ad hoc,' Trustee Coil moved and Trustee Stoel seconded creation of a special park committee. The motion appeared to pass though the transcript ends mid-discussion on committee size; no final roll call vote was captured.

publicComment: Three residents spoke during public comment. Patty Tulkowski (2153 Peach Brook, Plover) expressed strong support for Garrett Lezmi for the vacant trustee position, citing his professional engineering background and participation on the CLIP committee. Tim Stercora (2023 Greenbud Road) urged the board to move forward on the already-approved Leah Road trails project, arguing it has been delayed for four years and that water utility safety objections are unfounded since people have walked past those buildings for years with no issues. Bernie Kramer (Oil 150 East State Highway 153, Bethlehem) criticized the village's audit findings, noting deficiencies have grown from three to six over time and questioning whether the village will ever achieve accountability and regain citizen trust.

title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Public Works Committee held its regular June 8, 2026 meeting, conducting a public hearing on a special assessment for driveway approach construction at the Alderson Street and Jelnick Avenue roundabout intersection project, and approving multiple resolutions and contracts related to ongoing capital improvement projects. Key actions included approving special assessment authorizing resolutions for both the Bladel Avenue and Alderson/Jelnick projects, awarding topsoil screening and paving contracts, and approving a right-of-way services contract amendment and a change order for the Alderson/Jelnick intersection project. Residents near the Alderson/Jelnick intersection, which closed the morning of the meeting, can expect construction through mid-August 2026.

discussions.item: Public Hearing: Alderson Street and Jelnick Avenue special assessment for driveway approach construction
discussions.body: Village Engineer Michael presented the project overview for the planned compact roundabout replacing the four-way stop at Alderson Street and Jelnick Avenue, with a total estimated project cost of approximately $1.08 million ($900K construction, $180K engineering). Only two private properties are subject to special assessment for driveway approaches, with an assessment rate of $8.61 per square foot — Legacy LLC at $1,747.83 for 203 sq ft and McDonald at $1,549.80 for 180 sq ft. Committee members engaged in extended discussion about the large cost discrepancy between the $8.61/sq ft rate on this project versus $5.40/sq ft on the Bladel Avenue project, attributed to the smaller number of driveway approaches (3 vs. approximately 40) and different contractors (Hos Construction vs. Melvin). The committee consensus, led by Chair Hushang, was to continue using actual bid prices rather than a standardized rate, as setting a fixed rate could shift costs unfairly to village taxpayers. No property owners attended the public hearing. The chair closed the hearing with no further public comment.

discussions.item: Item 7 - Approval of 5/11/26 Public Works and Utility Committee minutes
discussions.body: A motion was made and seconded to approve the May 11, 2026 meeting minutes. The motion passed unanimously on a voice vote with no discussion.

discussions.item: Item 8 - Approval of 5/28/26 Bladel Avenue special assessment hearing minutes
discussions.body: A motion was made and seconded to approve the minutes from the May 28, 2026 Bladel Avenue special assessment hearing. The motion passed unanimously on a voice vote with no discussion.

discussions.item: Item 9 - Acknowledge May 2026 water and sewer permits
discussions.body: A motion was made by the chair and seconded to acknowledge the May 2026 water and sewer permits. The motion passed unanimously on a voice vote with no discussion.

Engineer Michael provided a detailed update. On Weston Avenue, pavement settlement in sanitary sewer trenches from fall construction is being repaired — gravel patches have been placed and concrete curb and gutter repour was expected later in the week, with paving to follow. Committee member Hushang attributed the settlement to poor compaction by the contractor, which Michael partially attributed to the 20-foot depth and blasted rock conditions during mid-October construction. The Alderson/Jelnick intersection closed at 7 a.m. the morning of the meeting, with Hos Construction targeting mid-August completion and an incentive in place for early finish. Detours are in place via Park Ridge Avenue or Alterie/Newport. Bladel Avenue project is underway with tree clearing and temporary water setup. Concord and Bayberry project bids are opening June 17. Well 4 GAC tanks have been pumping since October; Well 3 iron/manganese treatment plant addition slab pour was scheduled for the following day. Bellwood Avenue water main project had a bid opening the following Wednesday. Hinter Springs subdivision had concrete deficiencies identified in a walk-through with Denyion that must be corrected before letter of credit sign-off.

Street operations supervisor reported on spring activities including street sweeping, curbside spring cleanup (requiring five staff across leaf bag and brush pickup operations), in-house crack sealing, fixing broken water stacks in the annexed Rothschild area, top soiling and hydro seeding at Yellow Banks, and milling and repaving around Sandy Lane. Shop repairs covered multiple units including bearings and seals on Unit 404, gearbox and driveshaft on Unit 1913, and carburetor replacement on Unit 22. Michael noted the wide range of funding sources — storm water utility, refuse/recycling fund, general fund, water and sewer funds, and parks — reflected in a single month's street crew work.

Utility Manager Josh reported that Well 2 Carrie Well rehab is nearly complete, awaiting a new pump with zinc sleeves and epoxy coating to improve longevity. Unidirectional water main flushing began in May in the northeast portion of the village. A sewer lateral was bored through by what is believed to be Charter Communications, and discussions are ongoing. Messelix lift station cleaning was completed with crew starting at 4 a.m. on the previous Thursday to reduce flows. An INI (inflow and infiltration) study with InPipe is expected to be scheduled by end of June, with the goal of identifying sources of high wet-weather flows — total wastewater flows dropped from 53.8 million gallons in April to 37.5 million in May. GIS integration with IT Pipes televising data is nearing completion.

discussions.item: Item 13 - Resolution 2026-016: 2025 Compliance Maintenance Annual Report (CMAR) for Weston Sewer Utility
discussions.body: Josh noted the village typically earns top marks on the self-evaluation report due to no overflows or backups. A motion was made and seconded to adopt the resolution. During discussion, a typographical error was identified in the fourth 'whereas' clause — the word 'no' was missing, reading incorrectly as 'there are immediate critical problems.' A committee member moved to amend the resolution to correct the text to 'there are no immediate critical problems.' The amendment was seconded and approved unanimously. The original motion as amended then passed unanimously on a voice vote.

discussions.item: Item 14 - Resolution 2026-017: Bladel Avenue reconstruction special assessment authorizing resolution
discussions.body: Michael confirmed no changes from the May 28 public hearing report. The assessment rate for concrete driveway approaches is $5.40 per square foot. A motion was made and seconded to recommend the village board approve Resolution 2026-017. The motion passed unanimously on a voice vote.

discussions.item: Item 15 - Resolution 2026-018: Alderson Street and Jelnick Avenue intersection project special assessment authorizing resolution
discussions.body: The assessment rate of $8.61 per square foot, as discussed in the earlier public hearing, was affirmed with no recommended changes. Chair Hushang stated 'we should go with what is cost — it is what it is.' A motion was made and seconded to recommend the village board approve Resolution 2026-018. The motion passed unanimously on a voice vote.

Two quotes were received: PGA Inc. at $5.50/cubic yard (total $38,500 for estimated 7,000 cubic yards) and Warden Enterprises at $6.50/cubic yard. Michael noted the village saves approximately $100,000 or more annually by screening and reusing its own topsoil versus purchasing contractor-supplied material at approximately $20/yard. The village has an estimated 25,000 cubic yards of unscreened topsoil stockpiled. A motion was made and seconded to award the project to PGA Inc. The motion passed unanimously on a voice vote.

Two quotes were received for 3-inch asphalt paving: RC Pavers at $48,500 and American Asphalt at $56,465, against a budget estimate of $52,855. The overall 2026 surface maintenance budget remains under budget at $842,654 versus an initial estimate of $863,190, leaving approximately $57,000 contingency. Finance Director Troutman advised pausing additional spending to monitor fuel costs. In response to a question about quality, Michael stated RC Pavers has done good work in the village, citing a Kalen Avenue overlay from about 12 years ago that has far exceeded its expected lifespan. A motion was made and seconded to award the project to RC Pavers at $48,500. The motion passed unanimously on a voice vote.

discussions.item: Item 18 - MSA right-of-way services contract amendment number one for Alderson/Jelnick project
discussions.body: The original contract with MSA for right-of-way services was $28,425 based on three appraisals and three property acquisitions. During final design, additional property impacts were identified east of the roundabout, including a condo association with four separate property owners requiring individual title work and mortgage company approvals. The amendment adds $24,425, bringing the total contract to $52,850. Michael noted that completing the acquisition within a four-to-five month window — compared to over a year for the River Trail Estates condo acquisition for the Ryan Street lift station project — was a significant achievement. A motion was made and seconded to recommend village board approval. The motion passed unanimously, with one member noting they would abstain [noted after the vote].

discussions.item: Item 19 - Change order number one for Alderson Street and Jelnick Avenue intersection project
discussions.body: Michael explained that the bid documents inadvertently omitted a bid line item for 24-inch storm pipe that was shown on the plans, only including 12-inch pipe pricing. The change order adds $17,535 to correct this omission. Michael confirmed that even with this addition, the low bidder (Hos Construction) remains the lowest bidder by approximately $80,000 compared to the second-lowest bidder, and the total remains within the storm water utility budget. Unit pricing of $120–$130 per linear foot for 24-inch concrete pipe was validated against City of Wausau bid tabs. Hushang quipped the committee should tell the contractor 'we don't give him any more change orders.' A motion was made and seconded to recommend the board of trustees approve the change order increasing project cost by $17,535. The motion passed unanimously on a voice vote.

discussions.item: Items 20-21 - Future agenda topics: water main replacement and village lighting
discussions.body: Michael noted approximately 10 miles of water main replacement remain, even with ongoing projects, and the village is pursuing CDBG grant funding. On village intersection lighting, the committee discussed whether to proactively identify intersections needing lights. Hushang said he would take nighttime photos of intersections to evaluate. The consensus was that staff should act when complaints are received rather than proactively adding lights, as some residents prefer dark skies. The only recent complaint resulted in a light being added at Weston Avenue and County Road J. Michael will add intersection lighting back to the July agenda for further discussion, and Scoffield Avenue lighting was specifically mentioned as a continuation item.

publicComment: No public comment was offered. The chair called for public comment at the start of the meeting and also checked for anyone attending online; no one responded. No property owners subject to the Alderson/Jelnick special assessment attended the public hearing.

title: Police and Fire Commission Meeting
overview: The Village of Kronenwetter Police and Fire Commission voted 3-0 to reject a fire department applicant from continuing in the firefighter hiring process following a closed-session interview. The commission also elected new leadership, naming Commissioner Alex Vedvik as President and Commissioner Rick Smith as Vice-President. Approval of the April 27 minutes was tabled due to insufficient quorum of members who attended that prior meeting.

discussions.body: Commissioner Smith nominated Commissioner Alex Vedvik for President; no other nominations were received and Vedvik accepted. Commissioner Rule seconded. Newly elected President Vedvik then nominated Commissioner Rick Smith for Vice-President; no other nominations were received and Smith accepted. Commissioner Rule seconded. discussions.item: Closed Session - Fire Department Applicant Interview
discussions.body: Vice-President Smith moved, seconded by Commissioner Rule, to convene into closed session pursuant to Wis. Stat. 19.85(1)(c) to interview a fire department applicant. The motion carried 3-0 by roll call vote and the commission entered closed session at 5:09 p.m. with Chief Theresa O'Brien invited to participate. The commission reconvened in open session at 5:47 p.m. on a motion by Vice-President Smith, seconded by President Vedvik, which carried 3-0.

Following the closed-session interview of a fire department applicant, Vice-President Smith moved to not continue the applicant in the firefighter hiring process. Commissioner Rule seconded. The motion carried 3-0 by roll call vote, effectively ending that applicant's candidacy.

title: Community Life, Infrastructure and Public Property (CLIPP) Committee Meeting
overview: The Kronenwetter CLIPP Committee met on June 1, 2026, taking action on four key matters: recommending SD Allen Becker as general contractor for a new six-bay municipal garage (5-0), voting to proceed with Lea Road trails while eliminating the parking lot and driveway from the project scope to save approximately $17,000 (5-0), voting to recommend a parks subcommittee to the village board (5-0), and deferring the Chromewood Drive/Flanar-Gem Roth road rehabilitation project due to costs roughly doubling from estimates. The meeting also featured a public comment from a resident urging the committee to follow through on previously approved trail improvements.

agenda.item: Call to Order and Pledge of Allegiance
Roll Call
Public Comments
Approval of May 4, 2026 CLIPP Minutes
Police Chief Report

discussions.body: Trustee Patty moved and Garrett seconded to approve the minutes. No discussion was held. Motion carried 5-0.

Chief Terry (last name not clearly stated) reported an increase in complaints about e-bikes and bicycles in general, noting that staff are working to spread awareness about safe riding and rules of the road. A committee member asked whether the speed limit near a construction zone had been reduced to 25 mph; the chief was uncertain whether that was permanent or temporary.

The fire chief noted that April had been an extraordinarily busy month for both fire and medical calls, per the written report provided. No further discussion or questions were raised by committee members.

Public Works Director Greg provided an extensive update on storm and infrastructure damage. A severe storm knocked out power village-wide; flooding from roof downspouts inundated the WPS transformer at the municipal building, sending water into the main electrical panel in the police department. A transfer switch on the backup diesel generator also failed, leaving portions of the building without power. Greg noted a replacement part must come from Canada or North Dakota with a 7-10 day lead time. He also reported a lift station basement backup and pump issues at multiple stations. In response to a question about long-term solutions, Greg indicated that a Capital Improvement Plan (CIP) would be presented next month addressing aging infrastructure including original 1984 boilers.

discussions.item: Chromewood Drive/Flanar-Gem Roth Road Rehabilitation Bids
discussions.body: Public Works Director Greg and Village Administrator John explained that bids came back far higher than the originally estimated $620,000, roughly doubling in cost. After reviewing available TID funds, only about $61 in TID2 contingency and $27,000 in non-TID funds would remain, which was deemed insufficient given an anticipated change order on the Kronenwetter Drive project. John noted that borrowing proceeds must be spent by end of October 2027, so the funds can remain available. However, he cautioned that if the project is deferred, any future funding will be village general obligation debt rather than TID district debt, since the TID district can no longer take on new debt. The committee discussed potentially bundling the project with the Maple Ridge road project for better bid pricing. No vote was taken; the project was deferred pending further review of options.

Public Works Director Greg presented an RFP process in which six contractors responded and only SD Allen Becker of Athens, Wisconsin came in person for a full site meeting. Greg recommended SD Allen Becker as general contractor for a new six-bay garage budgeted at $800,000, which would house two police bays, two sewer utility bays, and two parks/public works bays. The two-thirds village share is already in the capital project fund and one-third comes from the sewer utility fund cash balance. Police Chief noted that the department currently has vehicles sitting outside, including one bay tied up with an evidence vehicle from an aggravated battery case. Greg also noted that ordering a sewer jetter truck before December 31, 2026 would lock in 2026 emissions pricing and save approximately $35,000. Trustee Patty moved to recommend SD Allen Becker as general contractor; Paul seconded. Motion carried 5-0. Timeline: design finalized in approximately one month, concrete poured in August, building erected in October, occupancy by January.

Committee members Jennifer and Paddy (Patty Kasky) reported approximately $3,000 raised so far plus a $1,000 donation of jewelry. Plans include attending the farmers market with a jewelry donation jar and being present at movie nights. The effort received coverage on Channel 7 news and generated social media activity on Facebook and Nextdoor. A committee member noted that residents at a recent bike-and-walk event expressed frustration about the park's name change from 'Sunset Park' to 'Bushka Park,' and described some equipment as outdated and potentially dangerous. No vote was taken; the committee agreed to provide monthly updates.

Trustee Erin (last name not clearly identified) proposed creating a parks subcommittee, citing three residents who expressed interest in serving after completing surveys at the bike-and-walk event. Village Administrator John noted that subcommittees are authorized under village ordinance, report back to CLIPP, and require board approval of appointments by the village president. John indicated existing budget savings from an unfilled planner/tech position and fewer-than-anticipated administrative committee meetings could fund the subcommittee without adding to the budget. Garrett moved to recommend the parks subcommittee to the village board; Dan (Trustee Muschka) seconded. Motion carried 5-0.

discussions.item: Trails and Leisure - Village Owned Property on Lea Road
discussions.body: Public Works Director Greg presented cost estimates of approximately $58,000 for graveling walking and bike trails on village-owned land near the Lea Road wellheads. He raised a security concern about increased public foot traffic near the wellheads and generators, noting that fencing quotes came in at approximately $45,000. Trustee Muschka had previously suggested scaling back by eliminating the parking lot and driveway to redirect funds toward the Bushka Park playground. Garrett argued that the committee and village board had already approved the project and that removing the parking lot rather than the trails was the right compromise, noting local residents primarily access the area on foot. He also suggested cameras (estimated at roughly $5,000) and door security sensors as cost-effective wellhead security alternatives. Garrett moved to eliminate the driveway and parking lot from the project scope and proceed with graveling only the trails; a second was offered (name [inaudible]). Motion carried 5-0.

publicComment: One speaker offered public comment. Tim Stracota of 2023 Greenbud Road spoke about the Lea Road trails project. He noted the project was approved by CLIPP in August by a 4-0 vote and by the village board 4-7-0 (likely 4-0 or 7-0; phrasing unclear in transcript), and expressed frustration at perceived backpedaling. He stated the project missed eligibility for a Noel Nelson Stewardship Grant for recreational trails because the state stopped funding and CLIPP had not acted. He recommended against eliminating gravel from the trail due to difficulty pushing strollers, erosion, and increased maintenance if grass is used instead. He suggested dropping the parking lot and driveway to save approximately $17,000. He cited a 2023 village survey in which walking trails ranked number one, selected by 54% of respondents, ahead of playground equipment and splash pads. He concluded by noting the trails are within a mile and a half of approximately 1,800 residential parcels and recommended requiring future developers to include parks or walking trails in residential designs.

title: RESCHEDULED Village Board Meeting
overview: The Kronenwetter Village Board approved five substantive items at its June 1, 2026 rescheduled meeting, including a concept plan to extend East Nick Avenue through to Highway J in coordination with neighboring municipalities, a police department policy contract, a municipal court fee increase of $10, and a Fire Department DNR grant application. All motions carried unanimously 5-0, with Trustee Stowell absent for most of the meeting.

agenda.item: Chairperson Comments
Public Comment
Administrator Report
Public Works Director Report
Finance Director Report
Consent Agenda: April 2026 Vouchers, ACH Transactions and Credit Card Statement Activity; May 11, 2026 Village Board Meeting Minutes
Fire Department DNR Grant Opportunity
Police CSI Policy Contract
Municipal Court Fee Increase
HR-005-Wage Adjustment Chart
East Nick Avenue Development

discussions.body: Administrator James Davel updated the board on recent flooding and electrical failures at the Municipal Building. He also shared information about a letter sent to the Marathon County Board regarding the Kronenwetter Police Department. Davel, Police Chief Terry McHugh, and Finance Director John Jacobs answered board questions about the Police Department budget.

Public Works Director Greg Ulman presented his report, including updates on lift station performance during the recent storm. Trustee Lesniak thanked the Public Works Department for their dedication during the event.

Finance Director John Jacobs reported that the 2024 Audit and an RFP for reassessment would be brought to the next board meeting. He also announced the Village would purchase Capital Infrastructure Project software to assist with CIP management.

The consent agenda, comprising the April 2026 vouchers, ACH transactions and credit card statement activity, and the May 11, 2026 Village Board meeting minutes, was approved on a motion by Lesniak, seconded by Myszka. The motion carried by roll call vote 5-0.

Gary Guerndt, Owner and President of PGA, Inc., and Kurt Suebert presented a concept plan to extend East Nick Avenue to Highway J, requiring coordination with the municipalities of Weston, Reid, Ringle, and Kronenwetter. Guerndt said the road extension would support future residential development and improve safety for current residents, with PGA Inc. financing construction and seeking reimbursement from municipalities upon development. Trustee Lesniak moved, seconded by Mortensen, to move forward with the concept plan and direct staff to work with the other communities. The motion carried by roll call vote 5-0.

Administrator Davel presented the item, noting the Kronenwetter Fire Department has received this DNR grant multiple times in the past. Trustee Coyle moved, seconded by Myszka, to approve the Fire Department's plan for the DNR grant. The motion carried by roll call vote 5-0.

Police Chief Terry McHugh presented the contract with CSI Policy, stating that updated policies would reduce liability, the cost is reimbursable, and the program would allow for efficient policy updates. Trustee Myszka moved, seconded by Coyle, to approve the contract. The motion carried by roll call vote 5-0.

President Joling and Administrator Davel presented the item, noting court fees had not increased in many years. Trustee Mortensen moved, seconded by Myszka, to approve a $10 increase in court costs. The motion carried by roll call vote 5-0.

Administrator Davel proposed revising the employee evaluation process so that managers are evaluated by October each year and the wage scale is set during the budget process rather than in January. Trustee Lesniak moved, seconded by Myszka, to approve the revisions to HR-005 Employee Evaluations. The motion carried by voice vote 5-0.

discussions.item: Closure of Municipal Center Offices for Administrative Work Days
discussions.body: Administrator Davel presented the current staff schedule and approach. Rather than take immediate action, the board moved to delay a decision. Trustee Lesniak moved, seconded by Mortensen, to postpone action until the first regular meeting in August, with the administrator returning with recommendations. The motion carried by voice vote 5-0.

Administrator Davel recommended four priorities: TID 1, changing Village culture, lift stations and long-term infrastructure, and the Capital Improvement Program. Trustee Myszka named parks, roads including Maple Ridge Road, business development, and TID 1 as his priorities. Trustee Coyle added that Village ordinances need to be reviewed and updated. No action was taken on this item.

publicComment: Teresa Holewinski, 1814 Forsyth Road, Kronenwetter, submitted written comments that were read by Village Clerk Jennifer Poyer and attached to the minutes. Holewinski raised three concerns: (1) the deteriorating and potentially dangerous playground equipment at Buska Park, which she said has been on the improvement plan for years, as well as a broken Eagle Scout book library bench at the park; (2) an access path on the north side of Towering Pines park that she said was excavated and converted into uneven terrain by the Evergreen Bike Group, making it inaccessible to seniors, people with disabilities, and stroller users; and (3) a request for a published date for a CLIPP committee walkthrough of neighborhood parks to allow resident participation, as has been done in past years.
actionItems: Staff directed to work with the municipalities of Weston, Reid, Ringle, and Kronenwetter on the East Nick Avenue to Highway J road extension concept plan.
Fire Department authorized to proceed with DNR grant application.
Contract with CSI Policy for police department policy updates approved and to be executed.
Municipal court costs increased by $10 effective upon approval.
HR-005 Employee Evaluations policy revised to move manager evaluations to October and wage scale setting to the budget process.
Administrator Davel directed to return to the first regular board meeting in August with recommendations on closure of Municipal Center offices for administrative work days.
2024 Audit and RFP for reassessment to be placed on the next board meeting agenda.

title: Parks Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Parks Committee approved installation of kayak launch signs along the Eau Claire River, deferred discussion on allowing dogs at the aquatic center until next month when the committee chair can be present, and received updates on Kennedy Park fundraising and Yellow Banks disc golf course improvements. The meeting included public comment critical of the dog day proposal and questions about Kennedy Park financial transparency.

discussions.body: A motion was made and seconded to approve the minutes from the previous meeting. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

Staff presented a timeline document showing the Kennedy Park master plan process from 2023 to present, including the May 9th ribbon cutting for youth baseball fields. Sean Oster explained that the Friends of Kennedy Park are currently fundraising for lights on the upper field, with approximately $85,000 at the community foundation and a goal of $117,000. Committee member Scott asked clarifying questions about village capital funds, which are currently not earmarked pending fundraising progress. A committee member noted the Friends group plans a public check presentation at their June tournament. Discussion emphasized that master plans are guidance documents subject to change and that the 10-year timeline allows flexibility.

discussions.item: Yellow Banks disc golf course update
discussions.body: Dan presented photos and maps showing tree removal locations along the river and wood mat installation over gas pipelines to protect them during equipment access. The mats are now village property with extras available for other projects. Committee members asked clarifying questions about the 8x8 wood mats and installation process.

discussions.item: Dog Day at the aquatic center
discussions.body: The committee decided to defer this item to the next meeting because Chair Katrina, who requested the item, was absent due to an unforeseen issue. The resident who spoke during public comment against the proposal was informed his objection was noted. No formal motion was required for deferral.

discussions.item: Graffiti on the Eau Claire River Trail
discussions.body: Discussion focused on challenges removing graffiti from concrete and memorial benches on the trail. Staff explained that pressure washer access is limited due to bridge weight restrictions and terrain. Options discussed included anti-vandalism coatings, seal coating, and potentially organizing volunteer workdays. Committee members suggested community groups might help with maintenance. No formal action was taken; staff will explore cleaning options.

Dan presented version 7 of signage for kayak launches along the Eau Claire River. Signs will be installed at three Weston locations (Highway J, Ross Avenue, and Yellow Banks) plus Schofield locations. The Marathon County Parks Foundation is funding the signs through state grants. Dan noted the Highway J launch may be closed all season due to bridge replacement. A motion to approve installation was made and seconded, passing unanimously.

Staff proposed adding food allergy awareness signage at playgrounds after receiving a request from a community member. The committee agreed to bring it back for further discussion. Staff also announced the Mock Miller Park master plan process will begin with a kickoff meeting with Rettler, and noted the June 11th open house for the bike and pedestrian plan.

publicComment: Two residents provided public comment. Andy (6504 [street inaudible]) spoke against allowing dogs at the public pool, stating 'There should not be animals allowed into a public pool. Period.' Jim Pensan (5002 [street] Weston), appearing online under the name 'Vern M,' criticized the 4 p.m. meeting time as preventing public participation and questioned Kennedy Park financial reporting, calling the $8,700 expenditure figure 'too coincidental' and demanding to know where grant money and glow event funds are located.

title: Regular School Board Meeting
overview: The DC Everest School Board held its May 2026 regular meeting, conducting officer elections, approving several financial resolutions including a bond defeasance saving over $900,000, and receiving updates on post-tornado recovery, long-range facilities planning, and the transition of the IDEA Charter School to a district program. The board also approved meal price increases for 2026-27 and amended a personal communication device policy during its first reading.

discussions.body: The board elected new officers by voice vote for the 2026-27 year: Shannon Grabco as President, Katie as Vice President, Ye (Len Jang) as Treasurer, and Ben Blivven as Secretary. All passed unanimously by voice vote. Outgoing President Lindsay Litzky was presented with a ceremonial gavel in recognition of her service from 2022-2026, with the comment 'the gavel moves next door.'

discussions.item: IDEA Charter School Transition to District Program
discussions.body: IDEA Governance Council President Noel Phelps presented to the board, explaining that the charter school's contract expires June 2026, and the governance council voted unanimously in April not to renew the DPI charter. Reasons cited included declining enrollment at IDEA and districtwide, the resignation of two key staff members, and the opportunity to offer project-based learning to more students as a district program. Dr. Lindell emphasized the district's ongoing commitment to project-based learning and noted the transition is informational at this stage, with planning underway for what the program will look like going forward.

Dr. Mike Raither presented survey results from 242 certified staff respondents (50% elementary, 50% secondary). Key findings included: 92% of staff believe DC Everest salaries are comparable or higher than area districts; however, staff expressed concern about salary compression as base wages rise faster than the full scale, and DC's top-end salary of $82,200 is below the local average of $83,540. Staff valued predictability, PTO, and professional development tied to the compensation model, but found PD requirements burdensome. Dr. Nye acknowledged the tension of studying compensation amid enrollment decline and facility planning pressures, but stressed it had been a decade since the last review. The board heard this as informational only; adjustments are planned to be brought back for board consideration in November-December 2026.

Business Director Kelly presented the resolution to defease a portion of the 2018 general obligation bonds, directing approximately $1.5 million of the 2028 maturity, $1.41 million of the 2033 maturity, and remaining 2034 maturity to an escrow account for early redemption on April 1, 2027. Kelly noted anticipated savings of just over $900,000 this year and over $10.7 million in defeasance over the past six years. The resolution passed unanimously 5-0 on a roll call vote (Larry, Katie, Lindsay, Jennifer, and board chair all voted yes; Ye and Ben were excused).

discussions.item: First Reading of Policies - Personal Communication Device Policy
discussions.body: Board member Larry raised a contradiction in the personal communication device policy: the first paragraph on page two listed after-school activities as a permissible time to use phones, while a green-text addition in the fifth paragraph stated phones must be powered off during after-school activities. After discussion, Dr. Nye clarified the statute (Act 42) focuses on instructional time and the added language was unnecessary. Larry moved to strike the contradictory paragraph ('During after school activities, PCDs shall be powered off completely...'), which was seconded and approved by voice vote. The board then approved both policies on first reading by voice vote.

Nutrition Director Tisha presented proposed meal price increases, noting DC Everest's current elementary lunch price of $2.25 is the lowest among its 64-district co-op, well below the co-op mean of $2.99 and below Wausau ($2.95) and Wisconsin Rapids ($2.55). She acknowledged the increase was a 'hard ask' given rising food and gas costs. Kelly noted fund 50 has a declining fund balance and without price adjustments, a transfer from the general fund could be required. Board members expressed support, noting the quality and variety of meals. The motion to approve meal prices as listed passed by voice vote.

discussions.item: Superintendent Report - Tornado Recovery and Facilities Planning
discussions.body: Dr. Nye reported the tornado struck approximately four weeks prior (between board meetings), passing 'diagonally through our district.' He praised staff for centering students and families, getting kids to GTCC and then back to Riverside quickly. He acknowledged ongoing variability in individual family circumstances and noted major exterior and roof work is planned for summer. On long-range facilities planning, Dr. Nye outlined four key takeaways from Monday's workshop: integrating planning into continuous improvement work, broad staff engagement across all departments, a community survey through School Perceptions planned for fall, and a target of bringing a referendum resolution recommendation in spring 2027 (likely December).

discussions.item: School Resource Officer Access to Student Records
discussions.body: The board approved a motion to grant school resource officers access to student records through the 2026-27 school year as listed in the background materials. The motion passed unanimously by voice vote with no discussion.

Senior Eric Winger introduced four 2026 graduates earning Wisconsin DPI Global Certificates of Competence: Ella Budleski, Lana Gorski, Anna Jacobson, and Ava Schuler. Each student described their service learning projects: Ella ran Operation Christmas Child collecting over 150 shoeboxes over three years; Lana hosted a coin drive raising over $150 for UNICEF; Anna led 'Bubbles for Bubbler' collecting over 100 hygiene items for the New Beginnings refugee center in Wausau; and Ava co-led donation efforts for 'Making Wausau a Home,' supporting children through the New Beginnings organization. Certificates were presented to all four students.

title: Administrative Policy Committee Meeting
overview: The Village of Kronenwetter's Administrative Policy Committee held its first organizational meeting of the 2026-2027 session on May 19, 2026, electing officers and taking action on several financial and operational matters. Key decisions included approving applications for a DNR forest fire protection grant, a police department policy contract, a municipal court fee increase, and changes to the employee wage and evaluation schedule. Finance Director John Jacobs announced the long-awaited 2024 audit draft had finally arrived, with a village board presentation planned for June 8.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Roll call
Election of chairperson and vice chairperson
Committee duties, responsibilities, vision and mission
Public comments
Approval of April 21, 2026 meeting minutes
Finance Director report
Fire Department DNR grant opportunity
Police department policy contract with CSI Information LLC
Municipal court fee increase

RFP for property assessment and revaluation services

discussions.item: Election of chairperson and vice chairperson
discussions.body: Trustee Jessica Stole was nominated and elected chairperson by acclamation, but was absent from the meeting. Because Stole was not present, the committee proceeded to elect a vice chairperson. Mary Soulheim was nominated and elected vice chairperson by acclamation, and she presided over the remainder of the meeting.

Finance Director John Jacobs announced the draft 2024 financial audit report arrived the day of the meeting, and auditors confirmed they will present to the village board on June 8. Jacobs explained the audit delays were caused by a $1.2 million debt service funding error made by predecessors over two consecutive budget years, plus unreconciled bank statement discrepancies dating back before 2024. Trustee Guy Ferdell asked if the predecessor's financial mess was the primary cause of delays, and Jacobs confirmed it was, stating 'it's taken care of' and that the 2025 audit fieldwork is expected in July or August. Jacobs also introduced new account clerk Samantha Hice.

Chief [name not clearly stated] presented a 5050 matching DNR forest fire protection grant the department has received nearly every year for the past five or six years. The maximum village match is $10,000, which would be budgeted and returned if the grant is not awarded in October. The committee approved a motion to set aside the $10,000 match in the budget. Roll call vote: Ferdell yes, President Jolene yes, Clues yes, Soulheim yes. Passed 4-0.

discussions.item: Police department policy contract with CSI Information LLC
discussions.body: Chief [name not clearly stated] presented a contract with CSI Information LLC for a standardized police policy manual. The initial cost is $2,500 and annual renewal is $500, but both amounts are fully reimbursed through the League of Wisconsin Municipalities, making it effectively no cost to the village. Policies will be delivered two at a time for officer review and sign-off. Committee members noted the importance of updated policies for liability, particularly citing the domestic violence policy. Motion passed on roll call: Ferdell yes, President Jolene yes, Clues yes, Soulheim yes. Passed 4-0.

The committee discussed a proposed $10 increase to the municipal court citation fee, from $38 to $48, the first increase since 2013. Chief [name inaudible] and Village Administrator Jim Davil presented the item, noting municipal courts are not revenue generators and that rising operational costs justify the increase. A motion was made and seconded to recommend the fee increase to the village board. Roll call vote: Ferdell yes, President Jolene yes, Clues yes, Soulheim yes. Passed 4-0.

discussions.item: Wage adjustment and employee evaluation schedule change (AR-005)
discussions.body: Village Administrator Jim Davil proposed changing the employee evaluation and wage adjustment process so that all employees are evaluated in October and wage increases take effect January 1 with budget approval, rather than on each employee's individual hire anniversary date. Davil cited benefits including easier payroll management, full Wisconsin Retirement System benefit year, and better alignment with the budget process. Staff expressed support for the change. The committee approved a voice vote motion to take the policy change to the village board. Motion carried.

discussions.item: RFP for property assessment and revaluation services
discussions.body: Finance Director Jacobs presented a draft RFP for a full exterior property revaluation and reassessment, noting the village's assessed value has dropped from 99% of full market value in 2021 to approximately 66-69% in 2025, triggering a mandatory correction before the state Department of Revenue intervenes at higher cost. The reassessment must be completed by October 15, 2027 for December 2027 tax bills. Jacobs explained the average property value increase will be approximately 35-40%, but that tax rates will fall proportionally, making it largely a wash for most property owners. The committee discussed the timeline, noting the RFP would go out May 22, proposals due June 8, and the contract would go to the village board June 22. No formal vote was taken; Jacobs sought comments on the draft RFP before release.

discussions.item: Developing a plan to promote development within the village
discussions.body: Trustee Guy Ferdell presented a discussion memo proposing a coordinated development planning process involving the APC, utilities committee, planning commission, RDA, and village board to prioritize development projects. Ferdell suggested the village should target higher-value projects above $700,000-$1 million and consider covering developer soft costs as incentives for high-impact projects. Davil noted positive momentum including a developer presenting a concept plan to the planning committee the previous night that was received favorably rather than rejected outright, which he described as a significant change in tone. The committee agreed the village board should first set priorities at the next board meeting, after which committees would coordinate. No formal vote was taken; item is a discussion and next steps were assigned.

publicComment: One speaker offered public comment: Bernie Kramer of 2150 East State Highway 153, Brokaw/Kronenwetter area. Kramer raised concerns about two simultaneous audits suggesting the village is behind, the lack of an economic development director, the predominantly residential nature of growth (85% residential), the absence of major commercial development, and fiscal caution regarding spending. He referenced Weston's failed ambulance referendum and the closure of Rosholt's water park as cautionary examples. Vice Chair Soulheim noted Kramer exceeded the three-minute limit but allowed him to conclude under the presiding officer's discretion.

title: Committee of the Whole Meeting
overview: The Wausau School Board's Committee of the Whole met on May 18, 2026, and advanced several items to the full board, including a preliminary 2026-27 budget, significant employee handbook changes affecting new hires starting July 1, 2026, updated meal prices, a revised athletics and activities code, and approval to move forward with Miron Construction for the Riverview Elementary HVAC and roofing project estimated at $5.6 million. The meeting also featured a brief Excellence in Action presentation from Thomas Jefferson Elementary before the committee adjourned into closed session for preliminary discussion of potential litigation.

agenda.item: Call to order and approval of minutes from April 27, 2026
Audit of the bills
Excellence in Action - Thomas Jefferson Elementary (delayed, presented later)

Adjournment and convening of closed session regarding potential litigation

discussions.item: Approval of minutes and audit of bills
discussions.body: Both the minutes of the April 27, 2026 meeting and the audit of bills were approved by voice vote with no discussion or opposition noted.

Assistant Superintendent of Operations Ms. Channel presented the preliminary budget, noting it is based on January assumptions including a 2.63% wage increase for some employee groups, a 10% health insurance premium increase, and a 1.5% reduction to district building budgets. Board members asked about health insurance utilization trends nationally and whether the 10% assumption was still appropriate; Channel said she would follow up with M3 on current trend data. One board member, identified as Corey, asked what buildings were actually cutting under the 1.5% reduction, and the board requested that information be reported back. The motion to recommend the preliminary budget to the full board passed with one abstention.

discussions.item: Excellence in Action - Thomas Jefferson Elementary
discussions.body: Principal Megan Korak presented highlights from Thomas Jefferson Elementary, which was formed through consolidation of Grant and Jefferson schools. She noted both buildings received high impact scores from the state, that 37% of students with Personal Success Plans (PSPs) had been exited and 52% of second graders had exited PSPs, and that four special education students moved to 504 plans. She also shared community-building events and noted the school adopted two mascots, TJ and Oreo. A board member noted the high impact award had been discussed at both CESA 9 and CESA 8 meetings.

HR staff presented three substantive changes applying only to new hires on or after July 1, 2026: (1) a consolidated vacation schedule replacing multiple differing schedules; (2) holiday alignment so employees working fewer than 230 days per year receive six paid holidays rather than nine, while those at 230 or more days retain ten; and (3) health and dental insurance standardized at 85% employer contribution for all employee groups, with eligibility threshold raised from 20 to 30 hours per week. Current employees are unaffected and will receive individualized letters with an option to voluntarily switch to the new benefit package. Two additional housekeeping changes addressed teacher retirement language dating to 2005 and a shift for new administrator hires to a defined contribution retirement benefit. The motion to recommend the handbook to the full board passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Proposed Changes to 2026-27 Paid Meal Prices
discussions.body: Nutrition Services Director Kieran Foos explained the district is required to complete the USDA paid lunch equity tool annually. To meet the required $3.19 weighted average pricing, the proposed change is a 10-cent increase for elementary, middle, and high school lunch prices and a 5-cent increase for breakfast. The motion to recommend the meal price changes to the full board passed unanimously with no significant debate.

Dr. John Tomsky and B.J. Brant presented four to five changes including: clarifying the definition of full-time student by referencing board policies 5200 and 9720; updating habitual truancy language to align with Marathon County Truancy Court procedures; adding look-alike prohibitions for tobacco and drug products in addition to the existing alcohol look-alike clause. A board member raised questions about enforceability of the look-alike provision in off-campus situations, and Tomsky explained that ambiguous cases go to the activities council for interpretation. The board also discussed that middle school athletics now operate under fund 80, which allows participation by private, home school, and other non-public school students, prompting the policy 9270 update. The motion to recommend the revised activities code passed unanimously.

Both policy changes were presented as companion updates to the athletic code revisions already discussed. Policy 9270 was amended to clarify that the WIAA participation restriction does not apply to middle school athletics funded through fund 80. Policy 2431.01 was updated to align official board policy language with the revised activities code document. Both motions to recommend approval to the full board passed unanimously without additional discussion.

discussions.item: Riverview Elementary HVAC and Roofing Project - Miron Construction
discussions.body: Buildings and Grounds Director Hermanski presented a recommendation to select Miron Construction as general contractor for the pre-construction phase (75% to 100% plans) of the Riverview Elementary HVAC and roofing project at a pre-construction fee of $5,000. Miron's total estimated project cost is $5.6 million, including $5.2 million in direct costs, a 2.25% construction fee of approximately $118,000, a 4% inflation clause, and bonding costs. Board members questioned the inflation clause and whether the 2.25% fee would increase if project costs rise; Hermanski confirmed the fee percentage is fixed but the dollar amount could increase with direct costs. The motion to recommend approval of Miron Construction as the general contractor passed unanimously.

Immediately following adjournment of the regular meeting, the board convened a special meeting and voted by roll call to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statute 19.85(1)(g) for preliminary discussion regarding potential litigation. All seven members present voted yes: Sarah, Charles, John, Pat, Corey, Lance, and Jim.

title: Board of Trustees
overview: The Village of Weston Board of Trustees approved increased park and recreation impact fees, passed multiple ordinances including mobile food vendor licensing and e-bike regulations, and engaged in extensive discussion about parking challenges at the relocated farmers market at Kennedy Park. The board also adjourned the Board of Review to June 15, 2026.

discussions.item: Public Hearing - Park and Recreation Impact Fees
discussions.body: Staff presented proposed fee increases from $300 to $761 for single family units, $250 to $563 for duplex units, and $250 to $495 for multifamily units, based on the 2022 public facilities needs assessment. Jim Pitel commented that the village lacks detailed data on actual park maintenance costs. Trustee Barb Lobsinger supported the fee increase, stating it's justified as new families require more park space. Ordinance 26-010 was approved unanimously, and Resolution 2026-015 amending the fee schedule was also approved unanimously.

Chief Finkey explained that this ordinance creates a regional inspection agreement with Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Stevens Point, Clover, Riverside, and other municipalities. Under this system, when one department inspects a food truck and places a sticker on it, other participating communities accept that inspection rather than requiring separate inspections in each municipality. Ordinance 26-008 was approved unanimously.

The board debated amendments to section 1.08 of the elected officials handbook. Trustee Beck raised concerns that the policy language didn't match what was discussed at the previous meeting. After discussion, the board voted to strike sentences requiring health, safety, or welfare justifications, leaving a broader policy where members notify the administrator and chair 12 hours in advance, must be on camera, and cannot participate in closed session. The amendment passed with two no votes from Trustees Hermling and Lobsinger, and the amended policy passed with the same two opposing.

President appointed Stephanie Daniels (currently on Finance and HR) and Golden Mua to the CLIPS committee. Motion approved unanimously. The board also agreed to include committee applicant resumes in future board packets.

The board discussed challenges with the farmers market's new location at Kennedy Park after being displaced from the Camp Phillips/Schofield Avenue location when the property owner decided to develop that land. Trustee Beck expressed safety concerns about cars parking on grass along Alderson Street. Renee explained the market grew from 25 to 53 vendor spots and that vendors appreciate having their vehicles with them. The board acknowledged that if no suitable location can be found, the market may need to be suspended. Discussion was informational only with no action taken.

Village Engineer Michael Dolan reported that an engineering study showed the 85th percentile speed was 45 mph, which would support keeping the limit at 45 rather than the 35 mph the original designer recommended. He noted construction delayed collecting additional data but will bring the matter back to the board, possibly at the joint meeting.

The board approved a secondhand article and jewelry dealer license for Schofield Coin and Hobby LLC at 5703 Jamar Street. Chief Hunt explained this involves buying and selling precious metals with large dollar transactions requiring tracking for stolen merchandise. The motion was amended to require satisfactory background check completion and receipt of occupancy permit since the building is still under construction. Approved unanimously as amended.

publicComment: Jim Pitel of 52nd Street spoke during public comment, criticizing the $108,000 cost for outside engineering consultants when an engineering technician position remains unfilled. He questioned why Lisa Beck was introduced at a SAFER meeting as a board member before being officially appointed by the board. He also criticized the lack of detailed information on Kennedy Park funding in the board packet and called for greater administrator accountability.

title: Board of Review Meeting
overview: The Village of Kronenwetter Board of Review held its annual maintenance-year meeting on May 13, 2026, primarily focused on organizational matters, the 2026 assessment roll presentation, and preliminary planning for a mandatory 2027 property revaluation. No formal property tax objections were filed or heard. Finance Director John Jacobs and new assessor Justin Servin of Associated Appraisal Consultants presented assessment data showing the village's total assessed value rose modestly from $735.7 million to $740.3 million, while the level of assessment dropped to 66% of full market value — underscoring the urgency of completing a full revaluation before the state-mandated 2028 deadline.

discussions.item: Selection of chairperson and vice chairperson
discussions.body: Trustee Lesniak nominated Dan Jolene as chairperson, seconded by Trustee Quail (Coil); approved unanimously by voice vote. Trustee Mishka nominated Craig Mortensen as vice chairperson, seconded by Trustee Stoll; a motion to close nominations was made by Trustee Quail and seconded by Trustee Mishka, passing by voice vote with at least one abstention. Mortensen was confirmed as vice chair.

discussions.item: Approval of July 10, 2025 Board of Review minutes
discussions.body: Trustee Mishka moved to approve the minutes and Trustee Stoll seconded. The motion passed unanimously by voice vote with no discussion.

discussions.item: 2026 annual assessment report and assessor oath
discussions.body: New assessor Justin Servin of Associated Appraisal Consultants administered his oath of office before presenting the 2026 assessment data. The village's total assessed value increased from $735,730,500 (2025 final) to $740,340,200 (2026), a roughly $5 million gain. The level of assessment fell from approximately 69% to 66% of full market value, reflecting a stabilizing real estate market. Finance Director John Jacobs noted the only correction from open book was a $864 refund to Jennifer Feltch at 1861 Mike Lane after confirming no new construction occurred as of January 1, 2025.

discussions.item: Tax-exempt pipeline property at 2007 Old Highway 51 (Magellan/Williams Pipeline)
discussions.body: Jacobs raised concern that a pipeline property was removed from the tax roll in 2019 after generating approximately an $11,000 tax bill, with the village's share being roughly 20%. Servin said he found a note in paper records from the previous assessor citing state statutes as justification but could not recall the specifics without reviewing the files; he offered to research it during the meeting recess. Jolene recalled a contamination event around 2001 prompting area sewer installation and suggested the property may have served a public entity, which could have triggered exemption. The board agreed to research the paper trail and state statute justification, and Servin suggested that if the exemption is unwarranted, he could assess the property in 2027 and require the owner to re-file exemption paperwork with full documentation by March 1.

Jacobs presented a map showing three parcels that moved to exempt in 2019: the cemetery parcel and two southern parcels totaling approximately 37.38 and 26.33 acres respectively. Servin noted Wisconsin law allows a religious entity only 30 additional acres of exemption beyond the actual cemetery, and the combined acreage of the southern parcels alone exceeds that limit. Servin said he would need to research county records to find documentation. He referenced a similar case in the Town of Hayward where he identified 600 exempt acres affiliated with a hospital and added $24 million to the assessment roll. He noted he can only go back two years to collect omitted taxes (2025 and 2026 in this case).

Jacobs outlined a detailed timeline: issue RFP in late May 2026, board approval in early June, contract start July 1, 2026 with a five-year maintenance contract plus revaluation component. Physical exterior walkarounds of all properties would occur through spring/summer 2027, with the state's equalized value released August 15, 2027 used to finalize numbers. Assessment notices would go out after Labor Day 2027, with open book and Board of Review to follow before the October 15, 2027 submission deadline. Servin confirmed the exterior inspection approach using field teams with iPads, averaging 35-40 properties per person per day, with no drone use. Jacobs emphasized that the revaluation must be completed before 2028 or the state will impose its own revaluation at two to three times the cost.

Board Clerk Jennifer Poyer and Servin confirmed no written objections or formal contest filings were received for the 2026 assessment year. The assessment has been open for review since April 24, 2026. No hearings were scheduled and no subpoenas were requested. Servin explained the standard process: burden of proof rests on the property owner to disprove the assessor's valuation with documentation such as appraisals, cost invoices, or comparable sales.

Board members responded warmly, joking about adding a dunk tank or 100-foot slide to water day festivities targeting new board members or the superintendent. One board member specifically thanked Principal Dred for her leadership.

The consent agenda covering staff appointments, replacements, contract changes, separations, retirements, meeting minutes from April 13, 27, and 30, payment of bills, budget status, investment report, and donations from organizations including Horsman Neighbors, American Online Giving Foundation, Forest Park Neighborhood, Charles Berger, Josh Dwey, and M3 Insurance was brought to a vote. The motion passed unanimously with no dissent or abstentions noted.

The budget reconciliation plan, previously presented by Assistant Superintendent of Operations Elizabeth Channel at the April Committee of the Whole, was brought for board approval. The motion was made and seconded, and the plan was approved with one abstention noted by the board president. No substantive debate was recorded.

Tabitha Gundram presented the list of teacher contracts for renewal for the 2026-27 school year, noting that state statute requires contracts to be issued by the 15th. The motion passed with one abstention. No opposition or debate was recorded.

The motion passed; no vote count beyond 'motion carries' was stated, and no opposition was noted.

Superintendent Bushman requested a motion to enter closed session under Wisconsin statutes 19.85(1)(c) for final notice of nonrenewal and 19.85(1)(e) and (g) for contract evaluation. A roll call vote was taken and all eight members present — Sarah, Charles, John, Pat, Corey, Lance, Yao, and Jim — voted yes. The motion passed 8-0.

title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Public Works Committee approved several infrastructure-related items including a state transportation funding resolution, a recycled asphalt crushing contract with King Gravel for $126,900, and construction service proposals for upcoming road projects. The committee also directed staff to develop $100/day fine language for sanitary sewer discharge violations after learning the village treated 15 million gallons of clear water in April due to illegal sump pump connections.

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a Local Road Improvement Grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Schofield Avenue and Community Center Drive, a project originally planned for 2029 that will now likely move to 2028. The grant covers about 50% of the estimated $1.5 million project cost. Michael noted the village has received over $9 million in grant funding over the past 4-5 years. Discussion also covered upcoming repairs on Weston Avenue by Integrity Grading and Excavating to address road depressions over sewer trenches, and planned repairs on Schofield Avenue by HOS Construction.

Staff reported on spring activities including road limit removal on April 29th, mailbox and sign repairs, dewatering operations near Weston Avenue tributary for about a week, curb line sweeping, and hot mix patching. Several culverts failed on Jones Street requiring replacement. The committee discussed the ongoing flooding issues near Crane Meadows and the need for a permanent pump solution rather than mobilizing equipment each time.

Josh reported on a service repair at Caribou Coffee, ongoing well maintenance, and significant clear water infiltration issues. April data showed 37 million gallons pumped into the system but 53-54 million gallons treated at the wastewater plant - approximately 15 million gallons of clear water. The Kathleen lift station was identified as having significant issues from sump pump discharges. The committee discussed the upcoming INI study by Inpipe Technologies to identify problem areas.

Michael presented the annual Public Service Commission report showing operating revenues of approximately $3 million and net operating income of $482,911 for 2025. Water loss was 6%. The utility value increased to nearly $34 million with $6.7 million in infrastructure placed in service. The utility has approximately 6,100 customers total. Motion to acknowledge passed unanimously.

Michael explained this League of Wisconsin Municipalities sample resolution supports long-term sustainable state transportation funding. He noted the village has decreased public works budgets recently and compared Wisconsin's automobile ownership costs favorably to neighboring states. Motion to recommend village board adoption passed unanimously.

Michael reported King Gravel submitted the low bid at $4.23/ton for an estimated 30,000 tons totaling $126,900. He calculated the village saves approximately $11/cubic yard compared to City of Wausau costs, resulting in about $130,000 savings across three 2026 projects. Motion to award to King Gravel passed unanimously.

The committee discussed enforcement options for residents illegally discharging sump pumps into the sanitary sewer system. Current code allows fines of $100-$1,000 per day per violation. Trustee Mumper suggested distinguishing between unknowing and willful violations. The committee agreed on $100/day as the starting fine amount. Motion to direct staff to create enforcement language with fines not exceeding $100/day passed unanimously.

Michael requested approval for a temporary lateral connection for a new duplex at 2230 Radkey Avenue until the street is reconstructed. The committee added language requiring Radkey Avenue reconstruction no later than 2029. Motion to recommend village board approval with the 2029 deadline passed unanimously.

The committee approved an intergovernmental agreement with Village of Rothschild for the Military Road project. Weston's utility costs are estimated at $94,860 ($6,156 for sanitary sewer, $88,704 for water main). Steam Construction is the contractor. Motion to recommend village board approval passed unanimously.

Michael presented proposals for construction inspection services. For the Alderson/Jelinek roundabout project, MSA proposed $37,315 versus Clark Dietz at $48,910. For Bladel Avenue, MSA proposed $70,400 versus Clark Dietz at $73,730. Michael recommended MSA for the roundabout and Clark Dietz for Bladel due to having full-time experienced staff rather than a summer intern. Motion to approve both recommendations passed unanimously.

discussions.body: The commission reviewed the site plan for the new police department and municipal court at 3600 Community Center Drive. title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an educational orientation meeting for new and returning board members, featuring presentations from department heads on the Health Department, Social Services, Veteran Services, and Aging Disability Resource Center. No action items were voted on except approval of the April 1st meeting minutes, which passed unanimously.

agenda.item: Call to order and remote participation confirmation
Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes

Veteran Service Department presentation by Aaron Galindo
Aging Disability Resource Center presentation by Executive Director Mike Ray
Next meeting date confirmation and adjournment

discussions.body: Supervisor Cavlli made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Vice Chair Rosenberg. The motion carried unanimously with all members voting in favor.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented the department's mission of preventing disease, promoting health, and protecting against environmental hazards. She highlighted over 1,200 licenses overseen, the new homeless system response position funded by the county board, public health vending machines providing free tick kits and harm reduction supplies, and the Nurse Family Partnership program which just graduated its first cohort of two-year-olds. Administrator Leonard noted the department has about 37.6 FTE and roughly half of their $5 million budget comes from tax levy, with significant funding from grants and public charges for services.

Administrator Leonard presented on behalf of Director Christa who was unavailable. He described the department's work in child welfare, youth justice, children's long-term support, economic support, and child support. The department has approximately 140 FTE and a 2026 budget of over $22 million with about $7 million from tax levy. Leonard highlighted the successful Family Keys housing stability pilot program, noting many clients served had never had a bank account, credit account, or been on a lease before.

Veteran Service Officer Aaron Galindo reported his 'small but mighty' team of 3.0 FTE had 8,149 interactions in 2025, up from approximately 5,900 in 2024. He noted $44 million in annual VA benefits flowing to Marathon County veterans, plus $2.6 million in retroactive payments in 2025. Galindo discussed suicide prevention efforts following 30 suicides in Marathon County in 2025 (7 were veterans), new housing stability partnerships with Center for Veterans Issues, and the PACT Act's impact on expanding presumptive conditions for VA benefits. Supervisor Kremes asked about regional coordination; Galindo explained state statute requires each county to have a VSO and described good working relationships with neighboring counties.

Executive Director Mike Ray presented on the four-county regional organization serving Marathon, Lincoln, Langlade, and Wood counties with about 60 FTE and 385-400 volunteers. He explained the ADRC serves as the one-stop shop for aging and disability resources and entry point for programs like Family Care and IRIS. Ray highlighted significant financial challenges in the nutrition/Meals on Wheels program, noting federal funding has remained flat since 2024 while food costs increased 40-45% over four years, forcing reductions in meals served, service days, and staffing. Marathon County's levy contribution is $413,135.
